4th-semester/bcc-208/solutions/ed-2024-e.title: ED 2024 Exam Q5
4th-semester/bcc-208/solutions/ed-2024-e.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/bcc-208/solutions/ed-2024-e.description:
To build a highly ethical culture, an entrepreneurial firm must establish a concrete foundation before scaling operations. The three primary prerequisites include:
Lead by Example (Tone at the Top): The founders and executive management must model ethical behavior in all their personal and professional actions. Consistent ethical leadership builds organizational trust and reinforces expected behaviors.
Establish a Code of Conduct: A formal, written document that explicitly details the firm's core values, compliance guidelines, and behavioral expectations for all internal stakeholders.
Implement Ethics Training Programs: Regular workshops and training sessions designed to help employees recognize potential ethical dilemmas and understand the proper procedures for addressing and reporting them.
An entrepreneurial firm typically manages its performance against four fundamental financial objectives:
Profitability: A firm's capability to generate revenue that exceeds its total expenses. Sustained profitability is required for long-term venture survival.
Liquidity: A company's ability to meet its short-term financial obligations. This requires active management of working capital and cash flows to ensure bills and short-term debts can be paid on time.
Efficiency: How productively a firm utilizes its available assets relative to its overall revenue and profit margins (e.g., optimizing inventory turnover and minimizing asset idle time).
Stability: The overall strength and vigor of the firm's long-term financial posture. A stable firm maintains a healthy, balanced debt-to-equity ratio and avoids over-leveraging.
A forecast is an estimate or prediction of a firm's future sales, income, and expenses. It serves as the baseline planning mechanism for operational budgeting and resource allocation.Firms utilize several quantitative and qualitative factors to construct accurate forecasts:
Historical Financial Data: Analyzing previous sales trends, expense cycles, and cash flow patterns (for established firms).
Industry Trends and Market Growth: Assessing broader economic conditions, sector growth rates, and changes in consumer purchasing power.
Operational Capacity and Intentions: Factoring in planned price changes, targeted marketing campaigns, production capacity limitations, and upcoming product launches.
Competitor Actions: Evaluating the entry of new competitors, expected market share shifts, and potential price wars.
The liability of newness refers to the high propensity for failure experienced by newly formed ventures. This risk stems from the fact that a new firm's team members must learn and adjust to unfamiliar roles, organizational systems take time to stabilize, and the venture lacks an established track record of trust with external stakeholders like customers, lenders, and suppliers.New ventures can successfully overcome this liability using the following strategies:
Assembling a High-Quality New-Venture Team: Hiring experienced managers, engineers, or operators who possess industry credibility and proven competence.
Appointing a Prestigious Board of Directors or Board of Advisors: Bringing in well-known, respected professionals whose presence lends immediate external legitimacy and strategic guidance to the venture.
Forming Strategic Partnerships and Alliances: Securing agreements with reputable suppliers, distributors, or foundational clients to signal stability to the market.
Firms prepare a pro forma balance sheet for several critical reasons:
Future Financial Positioning: It provides a projective snapshot of the firm's expected assets, liabilities, and owners' equity at a specific future date, ensuring the business is structured sustainably.
Structural Resource Balance: It confirms that projected asset acquisitions are completely balanced by appropriate levels of projected liability or equity expansion.
Investor and Creditor Assurances: It acts as a vital tool during funding pitches, demonstrating to prospective investors and lenders exactly how their injected capital will alter and optimize the firm's financial architecture.

4th-semester/bcc-208/solutions/ed-2024-e.title: ED 2024 Exam Q6
4th-semester/bcc-208/solutions/ed-2024-e.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/bcc-208/solutions/ed-2024-e.description:
Startup firms typically require capital investments for three primary reasons:
Cash Flow Challenges: In the early stages, a firm must purchase inventory, lease space, hire employees, and market its products before generating consistent revenue from customers. Capital investments bridge this gap between initial expenses and eventual cash inflows.
Capital Investments: Startups often need substantial upfront funding to purchase foundational assets such as property, manufacturing equipment, specialized machinery, or advanced information technology infrastructure.
Lengthy Product Development Cycles: For many innovative products or technologies, the development period can extend over months or years. During this time, the company incurs ongoing research and development (R&D) costs without receiving any revenue from sales.
Whether a firm can sustain itself without capital investment depends heavily on its business model:
High-Growth Startups: These firms generally cannot sustain themselves without external capital investments. Their strategy relies on rapid scaling, substantial initial infrastructure, and long R&D periods, making them heavily reliant on external injections of funds to survive their initial cash burn rate.
Traditional / Lifestyle Small Businesses: Some businesses can achieve sustainability without outside capital by utilizing "bootstrapping." This approach relies strictly on personal savings, lean operations, and immediate reinvestment of early customer revenues to fund steady, organic growth.
Entrepreneurs typically rely on three primary sources of personal financing to fund their ventures:
Personal Funds: This includes the entrepreneur’s own cash savings, liquidation of personal investments (such as stocks or bonds), or leveraging personal assets (e.g., taking out a home equity loan).
Sweat Equity: This represents the non-monetary value, time, and mental energy that the founder contributes to the business. It manifests as hard work invested to build the enterprise without drawing a market-rate salary.
Friends and Family: This involves financial support obtained from close personal relationships. It can take the form of direct loans, equity investments, or outright financial gifts to help get the venture operational.
Intellectual Property (IP) refers to any product of human intellect that is intangible but holds distinct value in the marketplace. It represents the legal rights resulting from intellectual activity in the industrial, scientific, literary, and artistic fields. It allows creators to protect their unique innovations and creative expressions from unauthorized commercial exploitation.The four key forms of intellectual property are:
Patents: Legal protections granted for new, useful, and non-obvious inventions, giving the inventor exclusive commercial rights for a specific period.
Trademarks: Words, symbols, names, phrases, or logos used to identify and distinguish the source of goods or services of one business from those of its competitors.
Copyrights: Legal rights that protect original works of authorship, including literary, musical, dramatic, artistic, and certain intellectual architectural works, once fixed in a tangible medium.
Trade Secrets: Formulas, patterns, programs, devices, methods, techniques, or processes that provide a business with a competitive advantage because they are kept confidential.
Case Scenario
Nusrat, a young entrepreneur from Dhaka, launched an online fashion brand named Heritage Threads, inspired by traditional Jamdani weaving. To stand out, she developed unique fusion designs blending Jamdani patterns with modern cuts. Soon after her business gained popularity, a larger fashion house copied several of her designs and marketed them under a similar brand name, confusing customers. Nusrat was frustrated as she invested heavily in design, brand identity, and online marketing. She realized she had not formally registered her brand name, logo, or applied for design protection. With sales declining due to imitation, Nusrat sought advice on how to protect her creations through intellectual property rights. She wanted to know whether trademarks, copyrights, or design patents would apply in her case and how these protections could help her business survive in a highly competitive Bangladeshi fashion market.
For Nusrat's business, Heritage Threads, the three most relevant forms of intellectual property protection are:
Trademarks: Crucial for protecting her brand identity, including the business name "Heritage Threads", its specific logo, and any unique brand slogans that distinguish her store from competitors.
Copyrights: Applicable to the explicit artistic expressions she creates, such as original textile illustrations, photographic promotional assets on her online platform, website content, and marketing materials.
Design Patents / Registered Industrial Designs: Directly applicable to her unique fusion apparel creations. This form of IP protects the novel structural visual configurations, lines, shapes, and modern cuts she developed to blend traditional Jamdani patterns with contemporary fashion.
Trademark registration will help protect Heritage Threads through the following mechanisms:
Exclusive Legal Rights: It grants Nusrat the exclusive right to use the name "Heritage Threads" and her logo within the marketplace, establishing a clear statutory ownership claim.
Prevention of Consumer Confusion: It provides a direct mechanism to legally stop competitors and larger fashion houses from operating under identical or deceptively similar names that mislead customers.
Enforcement Actions: It empowers Nusrat to file legal infringement lawsuits, claim damages, and secure court injunctions to stop unauthorized imitators from trading on her brand's established goodwill.
Asset Building: A registered trademark converts her brand identity into an intangible corporate asset, which adds financial value to her business and can be leveraged for future licensing or commercial expansion.
Nusrat should immediately take the following actions to safeguard her brand and designs:
File for Immediate Trademark Registration: Submit urgent applications for her brand name "Heritage Threads" and its associated logos to the Department of Patents, Designs and Trademarks (DPDT) in Bangladesh.
Apply for Industrial Design and Copyright Registration: Formally register the technical specifications and blueprints of her unique fusion design cuts and pattern combinations with the relevant IP offices to document priority of creation.
Issue a Legal Cease-and-Desist Notice: Consult an IP attorney to issue a formal cease-and-desist letter to the offending larger fashion house, demanding they immediately stop using her designs and confusingly similar brand names.
Publicly Establish Brand Authenticity: Use her online marketing platforms to educate her consumer base on how to identify genuine "Heritage Threads" products, while adding clear copyright notices (e.g., © Heritage Threads [Year]) to all digital platforms to assert immediate public claim over her works.

headers.heading: Relevant Forms of Intellectual Property Protection

4th-semester/bcc-208/solutions/ed-2024-e.title: ED 2024 Exam Q7
4th-semester/bcc-208/solutions/ed-2024-e.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/bcc-208/solutions/ed-2024-e.description:
Case Study: The Ahmed Family Handicraft Business
The Ahmed family has been running a traditional handicraft business in Kushtia for over 30 years. Initially started by the father, the business specialized in producing hand woven nakshi kantha and jute-based products, which became popular among local buyers and tourists. With time, the second generation-two sons and a daughter-joined the venture. However, conflicts have arisen regarding the business's future direction.
To resolve intergenerational conflicts and bridge the gap between artisan heritage and contemporary business demands, the Ahmed family can adopt several strategic management approaches:
Ambidextrous Organizational Structure: The family can structure the firm to simultaneously exploit its existing traditional capabilities while exploring new market dynamics. This means keeping the traditional core operation intact (to satisfy heritage buyers and tourists) while establishing a separate modern wing focused on innovation.
Dual-Branding Strategy: The business can preserve its core brand for high-end, completely authentic, traditional Nakshi Kantha and jute products. Simultaneously, they can launch a modern sub-brand targeted at urban corporate clients and international buyers, utilizing fusion designs that incorporate traditional motifs into modern products (e.g., contemporary laptop bags or modern home decor items).
Decoupling Product Design from Process Operations: While product design and craftsmanship retain their traditional, hand-woven authenticity, the operational processes can be modernized. This includes implementing digital supply chain tracking, computerized inventory management, automated bookkeeping, and advanced digital marketing strategies.
Collaborative Governance & Expert Advisory: Setting up a formal family board or council that includes an external independent consultant can help filter out emotional biases. This structured approach allows the first generation’s risk-mitigation wisdom to blend constructively with the second generation’s modern ideas.
Family businesses in Bangladesh are typically highly centralized around the founder, making transitions emotionally charged and logistically complex. Effective succession planning should involve:
Formulating a Family Constitution: The family must draft a formal, legally grounded document defining the long-term vision, ownership distribution, voting rights, and rules governing how family members join, operate within, or exit the business.
Competency-Based Role Assignment: Roles among the two sons and the daughter must be distributed based on documented capability and qualifications rather than gender or chronological seniority. For example, one sibling could manage supply chains and artisan relations in Kushtia, another handle urban and digital marketing from a corporate desk, and the third oversee financial strategy and design innovation.
Phased Leadership Transition: The father should gradually transition from an active operational manager to a strategic mentor or Chairman of the Board. This multi-stage handover (e.g., over 2–3 years) ensures that corporate knowledge, network goodwill, and relationships with local suppliers remain stable.
Professionalization of Management: Introducing non-family management professionals into key roles ensures accountability and helps standardise performance metrics (KPIs) for family employees, mitigating intra-family friction.
The case illustrates several critical structural and behavioral challenges facing micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) in Bangladesh:
Intergenerational Gridlock: A widespread lack of formal governance frameworks and succession planning causes internal friction during second-generation handovers, often leading to business stagnation or closure.
The Tradition-Innovation Paradox: Small-scale traditional entrepreneurs struggle to scale up because they find it difficult to modernize operations without losing the authentic artisan identity that defines their product value.
Informal Governance Systems: Reliance on verbal agreements and informal family hierarchies instead of institutional business structures prevents systematic conflict resolution when strategic differences arise.
Gender and Operational Dynamics: Effectively integrating daughters and sons into equal leadership positions can run counter to traditional socio-cultural norms in the Bangladeshi business landscape, requiring structured organizational adjustments.
To scale up globally without losing their core cultural heritage, the family can execute the following targeted strategies:
Story-Driven Heritage Marketing: Global ethical consumers value authentic social narratives. The brand should place the cultural background of Kushtia, the 30-year family legacy, and the individual stories of the artisans at the forefront of its international marketing collateral.
Fusion Design Partnerships: Collaborate with international fashion or interior designers to develop product lines that blend global functional utility with authentic Bangladeshi hand-woven textiles.
Cross-Border E-Commerce Infrastructure: Establish an optimized, direct-to-consumer (D2C) global website and utilize specialized international marketplaces (such as Etsy or global sustainable-fashion platforms) to bypass traditional intermediary exporters and retain higher margins.
Global Sustainability Certifications: Secure recognized certifications (e.g., Fair Trade, WFTO, or eco-friendly textile marks) and actively participate in international expos sponsored by the Export Promotion Bureau (EPB) of Bangladesh to secure reliable B2B distribution networks.
Case Study: Shahnaz & Women Empowerment in Rangpur
In recent years, Bangladesh has witnessed a remarkable rise in women entrepreneurs, contributing significantly to the economy and social development. One inspiring example is Shahnaz, who started a small handicraft business in Rangpur with just a few thousand taka. Initially, she faced challenges such as limited access to finance, lack of family support, and difficulty in reaching larger markets. However, through perseverance and training from a local NGO, she expanded her business, now employing 25 women from her community. Her enterprise produces eco-friendly products such as jute bags, handmade home décor, and traditional crafts, which are sold both locally and internationally. Shahnaz has become a role model, empowering women by providing employment opportunities and encouraging others to pursue entrepreneurship. Despite her success, challenges remain: accessing larger capital, competing with machine-made cheap imports, and navigating bureaucratic barriers. Yet, stories like hers highlight the resilience of women entrepreneurs in Bangladesh who not only uplift their families but also strengthen the nation's socio-economic fabric.
Shahnaz’s entrepreneurial journey underscores several distinct systemic hurdles encountered by women operating businesses in Bangladesh:
Socio-Cultural and Familial Resistance: Initial pushback from family and local communities driven by traditional patriarchal expectations that often view women's primary roles as domestic, casting doubt on their commercial capabilities.
Financial Exclusion: Extreme difficulty in obtaining formal banking credit due to rigid collateral mandates. Because family property or land is rarely registered in a woman's name in Bangladesh, female founders are frequently blocked from secured commercial loans.
Market Access Restrictions and Middlemen Exploitation: Limited physical mobility and lack of access to male-dominated wholesale trading networks often force rural women to rely on middlemen, who absorb a significant portion of the profit margins.
Unfair Market Competition: Handcrafted, sustainable goods are highly susceptible to market crowding and price undercutting by cheap, mass-produced synthetic imports.
Complex Bureaucratic Hurdles: Navigating the non-transparent paperwork required to secure trade licenses, tax registrations (TIN/BIN), utility link-ups, and export permits without institutional guidance.
Non-governmental organizations and targeted training programs act as crucial institutional catalysts for grassroots women-led ventures by providing:
Technical Skill Enhancement: Training programs help standardize manufacturing outputs, introduce quality control measures, and teach modern design principles, transforming raw domestic crafts into premium export-ready products.
Financial Literacy and Business Training: Educating women on foundational bookkeeping, basic pricing mechanics, capital budgeting, and digital literacy shifts their ventures from subsistence-level operations to organized business structures.
Alternative Financial Channels: NGOs frequently act as micro-lenders or credit intermediaries, extending collateral-free microloans or seed funds specifically designed for female entrepreneurs.
Network Building and Supply Chain Linking: They provide alternative marketing platforms and trade fairs that link rural producers directly with urban retail buyers and international fair-trade houses.
Psychological Support and Collective Resilience: Group-based training models cultivate a strong peer-support ecosystem, boosting self-confidence and providing social cover against local cultural discouragement.
Enterprises run by women, like Shahnaz’s handicraft startup, unlock extensive socio-economic multiplier effects:
Grassroots Employment Generation: Direct employment of marginalized community members (such as the 25 rural women in Shahnaz's firm) creates predictable household incomes, lifting families out of extreme poverty.
Intra-Household Empowerment: When women control independent revenue streams, financial resources are disproportionately directed toward family nutrition, healthcare, and higher educational attainment for children.
Shift in Social Perceptions: Successful female founders serve as local role models, dismantling structural gender biases and encouraging other women to pursue financial independence.
Revitalization of Green Industrial Sectors: Focusing on eco-friendly lines (e.g., jute bags and sustainable home decor) supports the national agenda for environmental sustainability and rejuvenates heritage sectors like the jute industry.
Macroeconomic Value Addition: These businesses boost gross domestic product (GDP), foster inclusive economic development, and expand export potential within the cottage and handicraft sectors.
To effectively scale up women-led enterprises across the country, structural policy adjustments are required:
Enforcement of Dedicated Bank Credit Lines: Bangladesh Bank (the central bank) can strictly mandate commercial banking institutions to allocate a set percentage of their SME loan portfolios to women entrepreneurs at preferential low interest rates, backed by relaxed collateral requirements and a robust state-backed Credit Guarantee Scheme (CGS).
Digitized One-Stop Regulatory Portals: Establish a streamlined, low-cost, digital "One-Stop Service" desk specifically managed by agencies like the SME Foundation or BSCIC to issue trade licenses, tax IDs, and export permissions to women-led startups without bureaucratic delay.
Targeted Infrastructure Allocations: Ensure that public trade events, domestic marketplaces, and international export pavilions systematically allocate dedicated booths and zero-cost display infrastructure to female entrepreneurs from rural divisions.
Targeted Tax Holidays and Fiscal Relief: Provide definitive multi-year tax exemptions for verified women-led green startups, coupled with zero-tariff import policies on primary manufacturing inputs for handicrafts.
Decentralized Incubation and Business Development Centers: Build fully equipped entrepreneurial incubation hubs at the upazila and district levels to deliver continuous legal, financial, technical, and digital marketing support to upcoming women founders.
