4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.description:
Sustainable development is an integrated approach to economic growth that emphasizes the long-term preservation of environmental resources and social equity, ensuring that the progress of the present does not jeopardize the survival and well-being of future generations.The most universally accepted definition was provided by the United Nations World Commission on Environment and Development (the Brundtland Commission) in its 1987 report, Our Common Future:
"Sustainable development is development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."
It stands on three interconnected pillars, often referred to as the Triple Bottom Line:
Economic Sustainability: Generating long-term economic growth, industrial progress, and employment without depleting the natural capital necessary for future production.
Social Sustainability: Promoting equity, inclusivity, gender empowerment, human rights, and the fair distribution of resources and public services like healthcare and education.
Environmental Sustainability: Protecting biodiversity, reducing greenhouse gas emissions, conserving ecosystems, and managing natural resources responsibly to halt climate degradation.
Adopted by all United Nations Member States in 2015 as part of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, the SDGs consist of 17 interconnected global goals designed to serve as a shared blueprint for peace and prosperity.
SDG 1: No Poverty: Eradicate extreme poverty (people living on less than target global thresholds) and establish robust social safety nets.
SDG 2: Zero Hunger: End hunger, achieve food security, improve nutrition, and promote sustainable, climate-resilient agricultural practices.
SDG 3: Good Health and Well-being: Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all ages by lowering maternal mortality, ending epidemics, and achieving universal health coverage.
SDG 4: Quality Education: Ensure inclusive, equitable, and quality primary, secondary, and vocational education, eliminating gender and wealth disparities.
SDG 5: Gender Equality: Achieve gender equality and empower all women and girls by eliminating discrimination, violence, and harmful traditional practices.
SDG 6: Clean Water and Sanitation: Ensure universal access to safe, affordable drinking water and adequate sanitation facilities while protecting water-related ecosystems.
SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy: Ensure access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy, heavily expanding the share of renewable energy sources.
SDG 8: Decent Work and Economic Growth: Promote sustained, inclusive economic growth, full and productive employment, and decent work conditions for all without exploiting the environment.
SDG 9: Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure: Build resilient infrastructure, promote inclusive and sustainable industrialization, and foster technological innovation.
SDG 10: Reduced Inequalities: Reduce income and opportunity inequalities within and among countries by adopting progressive fiscal and social protection policies.
SDG 11: Sustainable Cities and Communities: Make cities and human settlements inclusive, safe, resilient, and sustainable through affordable housing and efficient public transit.
SDG 12: Responsible Consumption and Production: Ensure sustainable consumption patterns by reducing food waste, managing chemicals safely, and adopting recycling/circular economy frameworks.
SDG 13: Climate Action: Take urgent action to combat climate change and its devastating impacts by strengthening climate resilience and integrating green strategies into national planning.
SDG 14: Life Below Water: Conserve and sustainably use oceans, seas, and marine resources by preventing marine pollution and overfishing.
SDG 15: Life on Land: Protect, restore, and promote the sustainable use of terrestrial ecosystems, combat desertification, halt land degradation, and stop biodiversity loss.
SDG 16: Peace, Justice, and Strong Institutions: Promote peaceful and inclusive societies, provide access to justice for all, and build effective, accountable, and transparent institutions at all levels.
SDG 17: Partnerships for the Goals: Strengthen global implementation frameworks and revitalize global partnerships through trade, finance, technology sharing, and data collection.
The Big-Push Theory, formulated by economist Paul Rosenstein-Rodan in 1943, is a classic model of development economics rooted in the concept of external economies. The central thesis is that a bit-by-bit or piecemeal allocation of investment resources is insufficient to launch an underdeveloped or Less Developed Country (LDC) out of stagnation. Instead, a comprehensive, high-volume minimum quantum of investment—a "Big Push"—is mandatory to overcome the structural inertia, break the vicious circle of poverty, and jumpstart sustainable economic growth.The theory uses an analogy of launching an airplane: a certain minimum speed and engine thrust are required for the aircraft to get off the ground; below that critical threshold, it cannot fly at all.According to Rosenstein-Rodan, this big push is required due to three critical indivisibilities in the economy:
Indivisibility in the Production Function (Social Overhead Capital): Infrastructure such as power plants, transport networks, highways, and telecommunications requires massive, lump-sum investments before direct manufacturing can become profitable. This infrastructure cannot be built piecemeal.
Indivisibility of Demand (Complementarity of Demand): Individual industries cannot grow in isolation because their workers do not spend all their income on their own products. For an investment to be viable, a broad cluster of complementary industries must be established simultaneously. This simultaneous expansion creates mutual markets, where workers from Industry A buy the goods of Industry B, and vice versa.
Indivisibility in the Supply of Savings: A high level of investment requires a corresponding high volume of domestic savings. Because LDCs have low per-capita incomes, savings are chronically low. A massive initial investment stimulus is necessary to lift incomes rapidly, which subsequently boosts the domestic marginal propensity to save.
Historically categorized as an LDC, Bangladesh has faced structural bottlenecks that align directly with the parameters of the Big-Push Theory. Over the past two decades, the nation's economic strategy has heavily integrated principles of the "Big Push" to catalyze its transition into a developing nation and aim for developed status by 2041.
Massive Investment in Social Overhead Capital (SOC): Bangladesh recognized that its industrial expansion was constrained by a severe deficit in infrastructure. To address this, the state launched high-volume public spending on mega-projects designed to generate structural external economies. Key examples include: The Padma Multipurpose Bridge: Funded largely by domestic resources, it directly linked the underdeveloped southwest region with the capital, slashing transit costs and accelerating regional GDP growth.
Dhaka Metro Rail (MRT Line-6) and Elevated Expressway: Massive capital injections to solve urban traffic gridlock, boosting economic productivity and factor mobility.
Rooppur Nuclear Power Plant & Matarbari Power Projects: Addressed the core production bottleneck of energy deficit, ensuring an uninterrupted power supply for industries. Complementarity of Demand via Special Economic Zones (SEZs): The government initiated the setup of 100 Economic Zones across Bangladesh (e.g., Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujib Shilpa Nagar). This mirrors the theory’s emphasis on simultaneous industrial expansion, grouping diverse, interdependent manufacturing sectors together to minimize supply chain barriers, exploit internal and external economies, and create robust internal markets.
Lifting the Country Out of the Low-Level Equilibrium Trap: The massive injection of public and foreign capital has successfully shifted Bangladesh from a low-income, foreign-aid-dependent agricultural base to a high-growth, manufacturing and service-oriented economy driven by Readymade Garments (RMG) and remittance. This has rapidly improved per-capita income and private domestic consumption.
While the Big-Push model has driven high GDP growth rates in Bangladesh, it has also brought along specific economic vulnerabilities:
Fiscal Deficits and Debt Sustainability: Financing these massive infrastructure investments has required large external and domestic borrowings, expanding the country's debt-servicing obligations.
Inflationary Pressures: The long gestation periods of mega-projects mean that huge volumes of money circulate in the economy before the actual goods and services start flowing, pushing up structural inflation.
Institutional and Administrative Capacity Constraints: Executing a successful "Big Push" requires highly transparent and efficient institutional governance. Project delays, cost overruns, and corruption in public procurement have at times diluted the net economic benefit of these investments.

The Big-Push Theory of Economic Development and its Application to Bangladesh
How Bangladesh is Affected by and Applies this Theory
Challenges and Pitfalls of the Big-Push Framework in Bangladesh

4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.title: BSED 2020 Exam Q4
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.description:
Vision 2021 was a comprehensive political and socio-economic manifesto articulated by the Government of Bangladesh with the primary objective of transforming the nation into a prosperous, middle-income, and technologically advanced digital country by the year 2021—marking the golden jubilee (50th anniversary) of Bangladesh's independence. This strategic roadmap was operationalized primarily through the First Perspective Plan (2010–2021), alongside the 6th and 7th Five-Year Plans.The core pillars and key focal points of Vision 2021 include:
Macroeconomic Stability and Economic Growth: Targeting sustained annual GDP growth rates of 8% to 10% to successfully graduate the country from Least Developed Country (LDC) status to a middle-income economy.
Poverty Alleviation: Implementing rigorous targeted strategies to reduce the head-count poverty rate below 15%, alongside widening the scope and efficiency of social safety nets for vulnerable populations.
Digital Bangladesh: Exploiting Information and Communication Technology (ICT) to modernize public governance, digitize service delivery, optimize transparency, and foster a robust, knowledge-based economy.
Human Resource Development: Enhancing the quality of institutional education, achieving a 100% literacy rate, and expanding access to healthcare, nutrition, and market-driven vocational skills training.
Infrastructure and Energy Security: Scaling up nationwide electricity production to achieve 100% coverage, diversifying into renewable and nuclear energy, and executing mega-infrastructure communication projects (roads, rail networks, and ports).
Good Governance and Institutional Reforms: Securing the absolute rule of law, strengthening anti-corruption mechanisms, promoting administrative accountability, and preserving an independent judiciary.
Food Security and Agriculture: Modernizing the agricultural sector through input subsidies, high-yield technology, and intensive scientific research to sustain absolute self-sufficiency in food grain production.
Climate Change and Environmental Sustainability: Building institutional resilience against climate change risks, environmental degradation, and recurring natural disasters through strategic adaptation and mitigation infrastructure.
The path to economic development in less developed countries (LDCs) is heavily deliberated across two foundational macroeconomic frameworks: the Theory of Balanced Growth and the Theory of Unbalanced Growth.
Core Proponents: Ragnar Nurkse, Paul Rosenstein-Rodan, and Arthur Lewis.
Concept: This theory asserts that an under-developed economy should undertake simultaneous, synchronized investments across a wide range of complementary industries—encompassing both consumer goods and capital goods sectors.
Mechanism: Because different sectors are mutually interdependent, the workers employed in one industry become the consumers for the output of other industries. This synchronous expansion systematically widens the market size, creates mutual horizontal demand, exploits external economies, and effectively shatters the demand-side and supply-side constraints of the Vicious Circle of Poverty.
Limitation: It takes for granted the availability of vast financial capital, technical capabilities, and advanced administrative execution, which are explicitly scarce resources within developing countries. Core Proponents: Albert O. Hirschman, Hans Singer, and Paul Streeten.
Concept: This approach argues that deliberate imbalances should be created within an economy to optimize development. Given that capital, skilled labor, and technical expertise are severely limited, simultaneous investment is structurally impossible.
Mechanism: Strategic investment must be concentrated heavily in specific "leading sectors" characterized by high linkage effects. Hirschman classifies these into Social Overhead Capital (SOC) (fundamental infrastructure, power, transport) and Directly Productive Activities (DPA) (manufacturing, factories). Strategic investment in SOC creates structural imbalances that naturally lower production costs, thereby inducing private investment in DPA via market incentives. Backward Linkages: Investment in an industry creates a strong demand for inputs from other upstream sectors (e.g., leather shoes demanding tanned leather).
Forward Linkages: Output from a sector facilitates downstream production industries (e.g., steel production boosting construction and machinery fabrication). For a developing country like Bangladesh, the Unbalanced Growth Model proves to be significantly more realistic, applicable, and effective due to the following structural dynamics:
Severe Resource and Capital Constraints: Bangladesh lacks the multi-sector investment capital and deep technical expertise required to develop all sectors simultaneously. Strategic concentration of capital is the only viable pathway.
The Ready-Made Garments (RMG) Catalyst: The economic trajectory of Bangladesh serves as a textbook example of unbalanced growth. The RMG sector was prioritized as a strategic export-led sector. Its massive rise triggered powerful backward linkages (spinning mills, accessories, textiles) and forward linkages (banking, commercial logistics, insurance, ports), pulling the rest of the national economy upward.
Public Prioritization of Social Overhead Capital (SOC): The Government of Bangladesh has actively practiced an unbalanced strategy by directing enormous capital into strategic mega-projects—such as the Padma Bridge, Dhaka Metro Rail, Rooppur Nuclear Power Plant, and deep-sea ports. These deep investments in SOC reduce systematic business overheads and act as magnets for private DPA investments.
Digital Financial Inclusion Ecosystem: Concentrated focus on digital communication infrastructure catalyzed the rise of Mobile Financial Services (MFS) like bKash and Nagad. This specialized digital imbalance successfully unlocked retail commerce, rural micro-enterprises, and agricultural value chains nationwide.
Conclusion
While a state of balanced growth represents an ideal ultimate target for long-term multi-sector harmony, the structural constraints and financial realities of Bangladesh dictate an unbalanced growth framework. By aggressively deploying scarce capital into priority infrastructure, energy grids, and export-oriented hubs, Bangladesh effectively generates the chain-reactions necessary to drive comprehensive economic development.

Applicability of the Models to Bangladesh

4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.title: BSED 2020 Exam Q5
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.description:
Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) play a vital role in the economic development of Bangladesh. They are often considered the engine of growth for their capacity to generate employment, reduce poverty, and foster industrialization across the country. The specific roles include:
Employment Generation: SMEs are highly labor-intensive and require less capital per worker compared to large-scale industries. They provide employment opportunities to a vast segment of the population, including semi-skilled and unskilled labor, thereby addressing the unemployment crisis.
Poverty Alleviation: By distributing income opportunities across urban, semi-urban, and rural areas, SMEs act as a direct instrument for poverty reduction, elevating the standard of living for marginalized communities.
GDP Contribution: SMEs contribute significantly to the manufacturing sector output and the national Gross Domestic Product (GDP), forming the backbone of domestic industrial production.
Fostering Entrepreneurship: They offer a platform for local entrepreneurs to innovate, experiment, and develop business skills with lower financial risks, paving the way for larger industrial investments.
Utilization of Local Resources: These enterprises primarily depend on domestically available raw materials and traditional skills, reducing reliance on imported inputs and preserving indigenous craftsmanship.
Subcontracting and Support to Large Industries: SMEs act as backward and forward linkage industries by supplying components, spare parts, and intermediate goods to large-scale manufacturing sectors like Ready-Made Garments (RMG).
Women Empowerment: A substantial number of SMEs are managed or staffed by women, promoting gender equality, economic independence, and social mobilization.
The Theory of Demographic Transition describes the historical transformation of countries from high birth and death rates to low birth and death rates as they move from a traditional, agrarian structure to a modern, industrialized economy.The transition typically occurs in distinct stages:
Stage 1: High Stationary Stage Characteristics: High birth rates and high death rates.
Economic Context: Agrarian economy with low levels of technology. Population growth is slow and stable because frequent epidemics, famines, and poor sanitation offset the high birth rates driven by social and economic utility of large families. Stage 2: Early Expanding Stage Characteristics: High birth rates but rapidly falling death rates.
Economic Context: Improvement in medical facilities, sanitation, food security, and public health leads to a sharp decline in mortality. However, birth rates remain high due to deep-rooted cultural habits and long-held social norms. This stage experiences a "population explosion." Stage 3: Late Expanding Stage Characteristics: Falling birth rates and low death rates.
Economic Context: Industrialization, urbanization, and rising literacy rates change the economic calculus of children. Children transition from being economic assets to economic liabilities. Higher costs of education and widespread family planning access cause birth rates to drop. Population growth begins to decelerate. Stage 4: Low Stationary Stage Characteristics: Low birth rates and low death rates.
Economic Context: Highly developed and industrialized society. The population stabilizes or grows at a very low rate, leading to an aging population structure. Eurocentric Basis: The theory is based on the historical experience of Western European nations and may not accurately represent the unique demographic patterns observed in developing nations.
Exogenous Healthcare Trajectory: In many developing countries, the decline in mortality rates was not brought about by indigenous economic development but by the rapid importation of modern medical technology and vaccines from abroad.
Varying Timelines for Birth Rate Declines: The theory fails to predict exactly how long a country will remain in the second stage or how fast birth rates will fall in response to urbanization.
Neglect of Migration and Disasters: It overlooks the impact of international migration, civil conflicts, or unexpected epidemics (such as HIV or pandemics) on demographic compositions.
Bangladesh is currently recognized as being in Stage 3 (Late Expanding Stage) of the demographic transition.
Evidence of Mortality and Fertility Trends: The country has achieved a remarkable success story in public health, dramatically reducing infant and maternal mortality rates over the past decades (transitioning away from Stage 2). Concurrently, due to effective nationwide family planning campaigns, increasing female literacy, and rising urbanization, the Total Fertility Rate (TFR) has dropped significantly toward the replacement level.
Demographic Dividend: Being in Stage 3, Bangladesh is enjoying a unique structural window known as the "demographic dividend," where the working-age population (15–64 years) exceeds the dependent population (children and the elderly). Effectively capturing this dividend through skills development and industrial employment is crucial for the country's transformation into a developed economy.

4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.title: BSED 2020 Exam Q6
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.description:
A Multinational Corporation (MNC) is an enterprise that manages production or delivers services in more than one country, maintaining its headquarters in a home country while operating wholly or partially owned subsidiaries or affiliates in various host countries. These corporations integrate global production networks, mobilize substantial financial capital, and possess advanced proprietary technology and managerial expertise.The presence and role of MNCs in a developing nation like Bangladesh remain a subject of significant economic debate. While they are viewed as catalytic engines for growth, they are simultaneously criticized for creating structural dependencies and wealth outflows.
Capital Accumulation and FDI Inflow: Bangladesh historically faces a domestic savings-investment gap. MNCs bring direct foreign equity capital, which supplements local investment without increasing the immediate external debt burden of the government.
Technology Transfer and Technical Know-How: MNCs introduce modern manufacturing processes, advanced machinery, and proprietary software. This technological spillover enhances the productivity of local auxiliary industries and modernizes the domestic economic landscape.
Employment Generation and Skill Upgradation: By setting up factories, corporate offices, and service centers, MNCs directly employ thousands of locals. Furthermore, they provide formal training programs that upgrade the skills of local engineers, managers, and technicians to international standards.
Export Promotion and Market Access: MNCs possess established global marketing networks. Their operations help diversify Bangladesh's export base beyond traditional items and integrate the domestic economy into global value chains.
Contribution to National Revenue: MNCs are often among the highest corporate taxpayers in Bangladesh. Their compliance with formal corporate frameworks ensures substantial contributions to the national exchequer via corporate income tax, customs duties, and Value Added Tax (VAT). Profit Repatriation and Capital Drain: A major criticism is that MNCs repatriate a vast portion of their profits, dividends, royalties, and technical fees back to their parent countries. This continuous outflow can exert severe pressure on Bangladesh's balance of payments and foreign exchange reserves.
Stifling Domestic Entrepreneurship: Due to their massive financial muscle, economies of scale, and global brand recognition, MNCs can easily outcompete and displace nascent local enterprises, leading to market monopolization.
Exploitation of Cheap Labor and Resources: Critics argue that MNCs are primarily attracted to Bangladesh due to low-wage labor and relaxed regulatory frameworks. This can lead to instances of labor exploitation, substandard working environments, or rapid depletion of natural resources (e.g., in the gas and energy sectors) without fair domestic compensation.
Inappropriate Technology Choice: MNCs often utilize capital-intensive production techniques developed for advanced economies. In a labor-surplus economy like Bangladesh, this limits their capacity to absorb the massive unemployed workforce, potentially worsening structural unemployment.
Political and Economic Interference: Given their economic scale, large MNCs can sometimes exert undue influence over domestic policy-making, lobbying for prolonged tax holidays, excessive subsidies, or lax environmental and labor laws that run counter to long-term national sustainable development goals.
Deficit financing refers to the practice where the government deliberately plans expenditures exceeding its expected revenues, bridging the fiscal gap through public borrowing, drawing down cash balances, or borrowing from the central bank (which often results in new money creation).
Infrastructure Transformation: Deficit financing allows Bangladesh to fund mega-infrastructure projects (such as the Padma Bridge, Dhaka Metro Rail, and deep-sea ports). These capital projects have long gestation periods but significantly lower transaction costs and crowd-in private investments once operational.
Mobilization of Idle Resources: In an underemployed economy, targeted deficit spending acts as a Keynesian stimulus. By investing in public works, the government generates employment, raises the purchasing power of citizens, and stimulates aggregate domestic demand.
Human Capital Development: When tax revenue is structurally rigid, deficit financing ensures that vital allocations for public education, healthcare infrastructure, and social safety nets (like cash transfer programs) remain uninterrupted. Demand-Pull Inflation: If deficit financing is heavily reliant on borrowing from the central bank (monetization of debt), it expands the domestic money supply. If this expansion outpaces the growth rate of real output (), it triggers high inflationary pressures, eroding the purchasing power of fixed-income earners.
The Crowding-Out Effect: Extensive borrowing from domestic commercial banks to finance the deficit reduces the credit pool available for private entrepreneurs. This competition for loanable funds drives up domestic interest rates, discouraging private investment.
Debt Servicing and Fiscal Stress: Accumulating substantial domestic and external debt creates a compounding debt-servicing burden. A rising share of the national budget must then be allocated toward paying interest rather than funding development initiatives.
Balance of Payments Strain: Increased government spending elevates domestic incomes, which typically translates into a higher marginal propensity to import consumer goods and industrial raw materials, worsening the trade deficit.
To prevent the harmful consequences of fiscal imprudence, deficit financing must be contained within distinct, structurally sustainable parameters:
The 5% GDP Threshold: Structurally, the conventional safe limit for the total fiscal deficit in Bangladesh is widely accepted to be 5% of Gross Domestic Product (GDP). Keeping the deficit at or below this target helps maintain macroeconomic stability.
The Productivity Rule: Deficit financing is safe only if the borrowed funds are channeled strictly into productive capital investments that expand the economy's supply-side capacity. If deficits are used to cover current revenue expenditures (such as administrative salaries, operational subsidies, or public sector bonuses), it becomes highly inflationary and unsustainable.
Source Optimization: The safety of a deficit depends heavily on its financing mix: Concessional Foreign Loans: Borrowing from multilateral institutions (World Bank, ADB) with low interest rates and long repayment periods is highly safe.
Domestic Banking Sector: Excessive reliance on commercial banks or direct central bank money printing should be minimized to avoid crowding out private capital and driving up inflation. Debt-to-GDP Sustainability: Total outstanding public debt must remain within a manageable threshold (typically under 40% of GDP for developing markets like Bangladesh) to ensure that future debt servicing does not compromise long-term fiscal solvency.

4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.title: BSED 2020 Exam Q7
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2020.description:
Economic planning refers to a deliberate, conscious, and centralized economic mechanism directed by a central authority to allocate resources, determine production paths, and achieve specific socio-economic objectives within a defined timeframe. The nature and scope of economic planning vary fundamentally depending on the prevailing economic system.In a capitalist economy, the means of production are privately owned, and the price mechanism (supply and demand) primarily allocates resources. Planning here is indicative and decentralized:
Mechanism: The government does not issue direct commands to private enterprises. Instead, it projects targets, coordinates expectations, and provides a macroeconomic framework.
Implementation: The state influences private decisions indirectly using fiscal, monetary, and trade policies (e.g., tax incentives, interest rate adjustments, subsidies).
Purpose: To reduce market uncertainties, stabilize the business cycle, and encourage investment in critical sectors without eliminating consumer sovereignty or private ownership.
In a socialist economy, private property is largely abolished, and the state owns and controls all material means of production. Planning here is imperative and highly centralized:
Mechanism: A central planning authority (e.g., Gosplan in the former Soviet Union) makes all fundamental economic decisions—what to produce, how to produce, and for whom to distribute.
Implementation: The plan carries the force of law. Directives, physical quotas, and production targets are mandatorily issued to state-owned enterprises. The market price mechanism is replaced by administered pricing.
Purpose: To completely align resource allocation with collective societal goals rather than individual profit motives.
A mixed economy features the coexistence of both public and private sectors, a model followed by countries like Bangladesh. Planning in this system combines elements of both imperative and indicative approaches:
Mechanism: The state directly plans and administers the public sector (e.g., infrastructure, defense, core utilities, public health) via imperative directives. Concurrently, it guides the private sector using indicative policy instruments.
Implementation: It operates through a democratic framework where national five-year plans or perspective plans set national goals, but leave operational freedom to private firms within a regulated environment.
Purpose: To achieve social justice, equitable income distribution, and rapid industrial growth while maintaining market dynamism.
For an economic plan to successfully realize its targeted outcomes, several structural and institutional prerequisites must be met:
Clear and Realizable Objectives: The plan must possess a well-defined, internally consistent, and prioritized set of goals (e.g., poverty alleviation, employment generation, industrial diversification) to avoid conflict in resource allocation.
Statistical and Data Accuracy: Reliable, comprehensive, and up-to-date statistical data regarding national income, population, consumption, savings, and resource availability are essential for making accurate economic projections.
Competent Administrative Machinery: A highly skilled, honest, efficient, and uncorrupted bureaucracy is vital for executing, monitoring, and evaluating plan formulations without bureaucratic bottlenecks.
Economic Balance Preservation: The plan must maintain critical internal and external balances to prevent economic instability: Saving-Investment Balance to control inflation.
Demand-Supply Balance to avoid commodity shortages.
Foreign Exchange Balance to mitigate balance of payments crises. Public Cooperation and Participation: Economic planning under a democratic framework requires the active involvement, confidence, and backing of the general public, local communities, and the private business sector.
Flexibility: A rigid plan cannot survive unexpected shocks (e.g., natural disasters, global recessions). The planning framework must allow for mid-term corrections and periodic adjustments.
Sustained Financial and Physical Resource Mobilization: Adequate domestic resource mobilization (through taxation and savings) coupled with optimal exploitation of physical resources is mandatory to minimize over-dependence on volatile foreign aid.
Technology transfer involves the transmission of technical know-how, skills, manufacturing processes, and machinery from advanced industrialized nations to Least Developed Countries (LDCs). While vital for accelerating industrialization, this process is fraught with complex challenges.
Inappropriateness of Imported Technology: Developed countries design technology to suit their own factor endowments, which are typically capital-abundant and labor-scarce. When LDCs import these highly automated, capital-intensive technologies, it often aggravates domestic unemployment and underemployment.
Exorbitant Financial Costs: The acquisition of modern foreign technology imposes severe financial strains on LDCs. These costs manifest as heavy licensing fees, royalties, patent fees, and expensive consultant charges, which drains scarce foreign exchange reserves.
Restrictive Institutional Clauses: Multinational Corporations (MNCs) frequently attach rigid strings or "tie-in clauses" to technology transfer contracts. These may include mandates to purchase raw materials exclusively from the supplier, prohibitions on modifying the technology, or strict limitations on exporting the finished products.
Low Absorptive Capacity: LDCs often lack the requisite technical baseline to absorb and domesticate foreign technology. A shortage of skilled technicians, structural engineers, and modern scientific infrastructure means that imported machinery cannot be maintained locally, leading to frequent breakdowns and persistent operational dependency.
Technological Colonialism and Dependency: Instead of fostering self-reliance, complete turnkey operations (where foreigners install, operate, and manage a plant) create a form of technological dependency. Once the foreign experts leave, local industries often find themselves unable to run or replicate the systems.
Dumping of Obsolete or Hazardous Tech: Developed countries sometimes use technology transfer as a mechanism to dump obsolete, inefficient, or environmentally hazardous machinery onto LDCs where environmental regulations are lax.
To maximize the developmental impact of foreign technology and avoid the pitfalls of blind duplication, Bangladesh requires a carefully structured Appropriate Technology Package:Core Philosophy of the Package
The appropriate package must not be a passive import of hardware. It must be an integrated mix of hardware (machinery), software (know-how), and "orgware" (institutional framework) tailored specifically to Bangladesh’s socio-economic realities. Labor-Intensive and Capital-Saving Choice: Given Bangladesh's massive labor surplus, the package must prioritize technologies that augment labor productivity rather than entirely replacing human workers. Examples include automated processing in the Readymade Garments (RMG) sector that still relies on extensive assembly operations, or mechanized agricultural tools suitable for small-holding farms.
Adaptable "Intermediate" Technology: Instead of shifting from primitive methods directly to hyper-advanced, computerized AI-driven systems, Bangladesh should adopt intermediate technology. This refers to machinery that is significantly more productive than traditional tools but vastly simpler and cheaper than advanced Western systems, making it easier to operate and maintain locally.
Unbundled / De-packaged Technology Acquisitions: Bangladesh should strictly avoid "turnkey" contracts. Instead, it must insist on unbundled technology, importing only the core specialized components that cannot be produced domestically, while leveraging local engineering capabilities for fabrication, civil works, and peripheral equipment.
Compulsory Skill-Transfer and Training Frameworks: Every major technological import or Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) agreement must include legally binding clauses for institutionalized capacity building. Foreign firms must train local engineers, managers, and technicians to handle reverse engineering, maintenance, and future adaptations.
Strengthening Domestic R&D Links: The technology transfer package must link foreign innovations with local research bodies such as the Bangladesh Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (BCSIR) and the Bangladesh Agricultural Research Council (BARC). This integration ensures that imported technology is systematically analyzed, broken down, and refitted to align with local climatic, environmental, and infrastructural conditions.

4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2021.title: BSED 2021 Exam Q1
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2021.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2021.description: Economic growth and economic development are two foundational concepts in development economics, often used interchangeably in casual discourse but strictly differentiated in academic and policy frameworks.Economic development cannot be completely captured by a single monetary metric. Economists utilize several sophisticated tools to measure it accurately: Human Development Index (HDI): Formulated by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), the HDI provides a composite metric based on three critical dimensions of human welfare: Longevity: Measured by life expectancy at birth. Knowledge: Measured by a combination of mean years of schooling for adults and expected years of schooling for children. Standard of Living: Measured by Real GNI per capita adjusted for Purchasing Power Parity (PPP). Physical Quality of Life Index (PQLI): Developed by Morris David Morris, this index measures development based on non-income components: Infant mortality rate. Life expectancy at age one. Basic literacy rate. Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI): Identifies overlapping deprivations at the household level across health, education, and living standards, offering a more vivid assessment of poverty than income-based lines. Real GNI per Capita (PPP adjusted): While an income measure, adjusting it for purchasing power parity provides a baseline measure of the real economic choices available to an average citizen. The Kuznets Hypothesis, advanced by economist Simon Kuznets in 1955, postulates an Inverted-U relationship between the level of economic development (measured by per capita income) and income inequality.According to this hypothesis: Early Stage of Development: As a low-income agricultural economy begins to industrialize and grow, income distribution initially worsens, causing inequality to rise. Inflection Point: Inequality reaches a peak at an intermediate level of development. Advanced Stage of Development: As the country reaches higher levels of industrialization and maturity, income distribution automatically becomes more equitable, causing inequality to fall. Kuznets and subsequent development economists identified several structural mechanisms that explain why inequality increases during the initial phases of economic progress: Inter-Sectoral Structural Shifts (The Dual Economy Effect): In the early stages of development, the economy undergoes a shift from a low-wage, traditional agricultural sector to a high-wage, modern industrial sector (similar to the dynamics outlined in the Lewis Model). Initially, only a small portion of the workforce transitions to urban industrial jobs, creating a vast income gap between modern sector workers and traditional agrarian laborers. Concentration of Savings and Capital Accumulation: In early industrialization, capital is highly concentrated in the hands of a small elite class of industrial entrepreneurs. Because rich individuals have a higher marginal propensity to save compared to the poor, they accumulate capital at a faster rate. This concentration of asset ownership compounds their income through profits and returns on capital (), widening the gap with wage laborers. Skill-Biased Technological Change: Modern industrial processes require specialized technical skills. In an early-stage developing economy, skilled labor is scarce, command over technology is limited, and the premium for skilled work is disproportionately high. This widens the wage differential between a small pool of educated/skilled workers and a massive pool of unskilled agricultural or manual laborers. Weak Institutional Redistribution and Safety Nets: In the initial periods of growth, public institutions are often underdeveloped, tax systems are regressive (relying heavily on indirect taxes), and social safety nets are virtually non-existent. Governments typically prioritize capital formation over wealth redistribution, leaving the lower-income brackets without compensatory welfare buffers. Urbanization and Urban Dualism: Rapid migration outpaces urban capacity, creating a dual economy within urban centers. A highly paid formal sector coexists with an unorganized, low-productivity informal sector (slums, day laborers), which drives up intra-urban income disparities.

The Kuznets Hypothesis of Economic Growth and Income Distribution
Causes of the Initial Increase in Income Inequality During Development

4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2021.title: BSED 2021 Exam Q2
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2021.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2021.description:
Bangladesh has transitioned from an agrarian economy to an increasingly industrialized one. The principal industries driving the country’s manufacturing sector include:
Ready-Made Garments (RMG) & Textiles: The backbone of Bangladesh's export sector, accounting for over 80% of total export earnings and employing millions of workers, predominantly women.
Pharmaceuticals: A highly sophisticated sector meeting nearly 98% of domestic demand and exporting finished formulations to over 150 countries.
Jute and Jute Goods: Traditionally known as the "Golden Fiber," this sector produces raw jute, jute yarn, twine, sacks, and diversified eco-friendly lifestyle products.
Leather and Leather Goods: A major export-oriented industry leveraging the country’s abundant livestock supply to produce leather footwear, bags, and finished leather.
Shipbuilding: An emerging heavy industry focusing on small to medium-sized ocean-going vessels, cargo ships, and ferries for both domestic and international markets.
Agro-processing: Focuses on processing food grains, fruits, vegetables, tea, and dairy products to serve both local urban centers and expatriate markets.
Light Engineering: Produces spare parts, electronic accessories, agricultural machinery, and bicycles.
Heavy Infrastructure Industries: Includes crucial domestic production setups such as Cement, Fertilizer (Urea), and Steel Re-rolling mills.
Despite significant growth in specific sectors like RMG, Bangladesh experiences industrial backwardness and a lack of manufacturing diversification due to several structural challenges:
Infrastructural Deficiencies: Power and Energy Crisis: Chronic shortages or erratic supply of electricity and natural gas disrupt production schedules and increase operating costs.
Transportation Bottlenecks: Inadequate port facilities (e.g., congestion at Chittagong Port), poorly maintained highways, and lack of deep-sea connectivity delay the transit of raw materials and finished goods. Lack of Adequate Capital and Credit Access: High interest rates on commercial loans and stringent collateral requirements discourage long-term industrial investment.
Capital markets (stock exchanges) remain volatile and underdeveloped, failing to act as reliable channels for raising long-term industrial equity. Technological Backwardness: Most small and medium enterprises (SMEs) continue to rely on obsolete or low-productivity technologies due to a lack of research and development (R&D) investments.
Slow adoption of Automation, Industry 4.0 standards, and modern machinery keeps production yields lower than regional competitors. Shortage of Skilled Labor and Entrepreneurial Skills: While raw labor is abundant, there is a severe deficit of specialized technical labor, engineers, and high-level managerial professionals.
The education system is poorly aligned with market-driven vocational skills, forcing heavy industries to depend on foreign experts. Bureaucratic Red Tape and Corruption: Complex regulatory frameworks, delays in obtaining utility connections, environmental clearances, and trade licenses create a high cost of doing business.
Inconsistent tax regimes and customs harassment discourage both domestic entrepreneurs and Foreign Direct Investment (FDI). Narrow Export Basket and Market Concentration: Over-reliance on the RMG sector creates structural vulnerability. Other potential sectors fail to scale up due to a lack of targeted policy support, fiscal incentives, and trade diplomacy. Bangladesh possesses modest but economically significant reserves of mineral resources concentrated primarily in its eastern and northwestern regions:Rapid economic growth, industrialization, and urbanization in Bangladesh have significantly degraded natural ecosystems, presenting a major trade-off between growth and sustainability:
Industrial Pollution and Water Contamination: Effluents from textile dyers, tanneries, and chemical plants are frequently discharged untreated into major river systems (e.g., Buriganga, Shitalakshya, and Karnaphuli).
This has destroyed aquatic biodiversity, created "dead zones" in rivers, and contaminated the food chain via heavy metals. Air Quality Degradation: Unregulated emissions from traditional brick kilns, fitness-lacking commercial vehicles, and construction dust have turned urban centers like Dhaka into some of the most polluted cities globally.
This drives severe respiratory illnesses, lowering human capital productivity. Deforestation and Land Degradation: Industrial expansion, commercial aquaculture (shrimp farming), and infrastructural projects have led to massive deforestation in the Chittagong Hill Tracts and the encroachment of the Sundarbans mangrove forest.
Soil fertility is depleted by excessive use of chemical fertilizers to maintain food security for a growing population. Groundwater Depletion: Massive extraction of groundwater for intensive agricultural irrigation (such as Boro rice cultivation) and heavy industrial use (RMG washing and dyeing units) has caused the water table to drop critically in urban and semi-arid zones (e.g., the Barind Tract). Solid and Electronic Waste Management Crisis: Urban consumption generates vast amounts of municipal solid waste and non-biodegradable plastic pollution that clogs urban drainage systems, worsening artificial flooding.
The digital transformation has introduced a massive influx of hazardous electronic waste (e-waste) without standard recycling protocols. Ecosystem Disruption due to Mining: Open-cast and underground extraction of sand, stone, and coal destroys local topsoil, induces artificial landslides, and leads to the leaching of acidic compounds into surrounding agricultural plains.

Mineral Resources of Bangladesh and Environmental Impacts of Economic Development
Environmental Problems Arising with Economic Development

4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2021.title: BSED 2021 Exam Q3
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2021.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2021.description:
Joseph Schumpeter's theory of economic development, primarily outlined in his seminal work The Theory of Economic Development (1911), shifts the focus of economic growth away from traditional static equilibrium analyses and places structural, dynamic changes at the center. Unlike classical economists who emphasized capital accumulation and population growth, Schumpeter argued that development is driven by internal, discontinuous, and revolutionary changes in economic life.Schumpeter begins with the concept of the "circular flow," a state of perfect macroeconomic equilibrium where economic activity repeats itself rhythmically year after year.
Characteristics: In this state, there are no structural changes, no net investments, no savings, and profits are zero (except for wages of management and ground rent). Demand perfectly matches supply, and every producer knows their market behavior based on historical experience.
Function: It serves as the baseline from which real economic development departs.
Schumpeter defines development as the execution of new combinations that disrupt the equilibrium of the circular flow. It is a spontaneous and discontinuous change in the channels of the flow, disturbance of equilibrium, which alters and displaces the equilibrium state previously existing.Development is driven by innovation, which is distinctly different from invention. An invention is the discovery of new scientific or technical knowledge, whereas an innovation is the commercial application of that knowledge. Schumpeter classifies innovation into five distinct categories:
Introduction of a new good: Launching a product that consumers are not yet familiar with, or a new quality of a good.
Introduction of a new method of production: Implementing a production process not yet tested by experience in the branch of manufacture concerned.
Opening of a new market: Entering a market into which the particular branch of manufacture of the country has not previously entered, whether or not this market has existed before.
Conquest of a new source of supply: Securing a new source of raw materials or semi-manufactured goods, irrespective of whether this source has to be created or already exists.
Carrying out of the new organization of any industry: Structural restructuring, such as the creation of a monopoly position or the breaking up of a monopoly position.
The entrepreneur is the central figure in Schumpeter’s theory. They are not merely managers, capitalists, or investors; they are visionaries who possess the capability and psychological drive to execute "new combinations."
Motivations: Schumpeter identifies three non-hedonistic motives for entrepreneurs: The desire to found a private kingdom or dynasty.
The will to conquer, the impulse to fight, to prove oneself superior to others.
The joy of creating, of getting things done, or simply of exercising one's energy and ingenuity. Because the circular flow assumes no savings or surplus funds, entrepreneurs cannot finance their innovations out of existing capital. Therefore, bank credit creation is an indispensable condition for development.
Banks provide newly created money (fiat credit) to the entrepreneur.
This credit allows the entrepreneur to bid resources away from the traditional channels of the circular flow and divert them toward innovative, high-yield configurations.
Economic development is not a smooth, linear progression; it occurs through cyclical fluctuations. Schumpeter explains this using a wave-like process:
The Boom (Prosperity Phase): When an exceptional pioneer successfully innovates, supernormal profits are realized. This breaks open new pathways, and a "swarm" of secondary imitators follows. Investment surges, demand for inputs rises, incomes expand, and the economy undergoes a vigorous boom.
The Bust (Recession Phase): As the new products flood the market, competition intensifies, and supernormal profits vanish. Simultaneously, entrepreneurs begin to repay their bank loans out of profits, causing credit contraction.
Creative Destruction: Old, obsolete firms operating within the old circular flow cannot compete with the lower costs or superior products of the innovators and are liquidated. This cleansing process, termed "Creative Destruction," forces the economy into a recession until it settles into a new, structurally advanced equilibrium position at a higher level of productive capacity.
While highly influential in describing the dynamic nature of capitalism, Schumpeter's model faces significant criticisms, particularly regarding its realism and its applicability to modern global contexts:
Overemphasis on the Entrepreneur: Schumpeter views the entrepreneur as an isolated, heroic figure. In modern corporate capitalism, innovation is institutionalized and handled by large R&D departments, teams, and state-funded laboratories rather than lone individuals.
Exaggerated Reliance on Bank Credit: The model assumes that all initial innovations are financed solely through inflationary bank credit. It overlooks the critical roles played by corporate retained earnings, equity financing, venture capital, and public fiscal interventions.
Inapplicability to Less Developed Countries (LDCs): The theory is highly tailored to advanced capitalistic societies. LDCs typically lack: A class of high-risk-bearing entrepreneurs.
Well-developed institutional frameworks and financial markets.
The capacity for fundamental innovation (LDCs rely heavily on adaptation, imitation, and technology transfer rather than pioneering innovations). Neglect of the Agricultural Sector: Schumpeter's framework is heavily biased toward industrial and commercial sectors. It ignores the fact that in many developing economies, structural transformation and economic development originate from agrarian productivity shocks.
Psychological and Unquantifiable Factors: By grounding the entire engine of development on the subjective psychological motivations of the entrepreneur, the theory becomes difficult to measure mathematically, formalize into public policy, or replicate through national economic planning.
The Harrod-Domar model represents a milestone in modern growth economics. Formulated independently by Sir Roy Harrod in 1939 and Evsey Domar in 1946, the model extends John Maynard Keynes’ short-run static macroeconomic analysis into a dynamic, long-run theory of economic growth.The foundational insight of the Harrod-Domar model is that investment plays a dual role in the macroeconomy:
Demand Effect: It generates income via the Keynesian multiplier process ().
Supply Effect: It increases the productive capacity of the economy by expanding the physical capital stock ().
While Keynes focused almost exclusively on the demand-side income-generation properties of investment to solve short-run unemployment, Harrod and Domar argued that for continuous, non-inflationary growth, the expanded capacity generated on the supply side must be fully matched by an equivalent expansion in aggregate demand. A closed economy with no government sector (). The savings ratio () is a constant fraction of national income (), meaning , where is both the marginal and average propensity to save. The capital-output ratio (), also known as the Incremental Capital-Output Ratio (ICOR), is constant: Capital does not depreciate, or depreciation is accounted for net of investment. Capital and labor are used in fixed proportions; there is no substitution between the factors of production. By definition, macroeconomic equilibrium requires that planned savings equal planned investment:Since savings is a function of income:The incremental capital stock () required to produce an additional unit of output () is governed by the capital-output ratio ():Because net investment () is simply the change in the capital stock ():Substituting the definitions of and back into the equilibrium condition ():To find the growth rate of output (), we rearrange the equation by dividing both sides by and :Letting denote the rate of growth of national income, we obtain the fundamental Harrod-Domar growth equation:Summary of equation
The rate of growth of an economy () is directly proportional to the savings rate () and inversely proportional to the capital-output ratio (). To accelerate growth, an economy must either increase its rate of savings or improve its capital efficiency (which lowers the value of ).
To evaluate long-run stability, Harrod broke down the growth path into three separate growth rates: Actual Growth Rate (): The real, observed rate of change in cyclical output over a given period, determined by prevailing empirical savings and investment ratios: Warranted Growth Rate (): The full-capacity growth rate at which producers are completely satisfied that their investment decisions match market demand. At this rate, there is neither overproduction nor underproduction, and capital is fully utilized: (where represents the required capital-output ratio to maintain entrepreneurial satisfaction). Natural Growth Rate (): The maximum potential growth rate achievable by the economy given its structural physical constraints, specifically the growth rate of the labor force () and the rate of technical progress (): For an economy to maintain a stable, non-inflationary path with full employment of both capital and labor, all three growth rates must perfectly coincide:However, Harrod and Domar argued that this triple equality is highly unlikely to occur naturally. Because the determinants of these rates (, , , and ) are fixed by independent institutional, demographic, and technological factors, there is no automatic market mechanism to bring them into alignment.This structural instability is known as the "Knife-Edge Equilibrium":The model has direct policy relevance for economies like Bangladesh:
The Savings Gap: LDCs typically experience low growth rates because low per-capita incomes lead to low domestic savings rates ().
Targeting the Gap: If a country wishes to achieve a target growth rate () but lacks the required internal savings (), it faces a "savings gap."
Remedying the Gap: Governments can close this gap by: Mobilizing foreign aid, concessionary loans, and foreign direct investment (FDI).
Advancing structural reforms to lower the capital-output ratio (), thereby increasing capital productivity through efficiency gains and better technology.

4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2021.title: BSED 2021 Exam Q4
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2021.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/bcc-210/solutions/bsed-2021.description:
Capital formation refers to the net addition to the physical stock of capital goods (such as machinery, equipment, buildings, and infrastructure) in an economy during a given period. It represents the process of diverting a portion of society's current income from consumption to investment. In the context of Bangladesh, capital formation serves as a critical measure and driver of economic development due to the following reasons:
Increases Productive Capacity: By building roads, bridges, power plants, and industrial infrastructure, capital formation expands the country's production frontier, enabling higher output levels across agriculture, manufacturing, and services.
Enhances Labor Productivity: Providing workers with better machinery, tools, and technical infrastructure elevates the output per worker, leading to real wage increases and higher living standards.
Promotes Technological Progress: Modern capital equipment embeds advanced technology. Therefore, the rate of capital formation dictates how rapidly an economy can adopt and assimilate modern, efficient production techniques.
Generates Employment Opportunities: The construction of physical capital and the subsequent operation of new factories and businesses create substantial direct and indirect employment, helping absorb the surplus labor force.
Breaks the Vicious Circle of Poverty: Low income leads to low savings, low investment, and low capital formation, which perpetuates low income. Accelerating capital formation breaks this circle by elevating investment levels and driving continuous economic growth.
The sources available to Bangladesh for mobilizing capital can be broadly categorized into domestic and foreign sources:
Voluntary Savings: This includes individual or household savings deposited in banking institutions, insurance policies, and national savings certificates, alongside corporate retained earnings reinvested into businesses.
Involuntary Savings (Taxation): The government forces compulsory savings by raising revenue through direct taxes (income tax, corporate tax) and indirect taxes (VAT, customs duties). The surplus of government revenue over current expenditure is directed toward capital expenditure.
Deficit Financing: When administrative revenues are insufficient, the government borrows from the central bank (by printing money) or commercial banks to fund development projects, acting as a forced mobilization of domestic resources.
Public Enterprise Profits: Surplus revenues generated by state-owned enterprises, statutory corporations, and utilities that are plowed back into infrastructural development. Foreign Direct Investment (FDI): Inflows of equity capital from multinational corporations investing directly in manufacturing, telecommunications, energy, and infrastructure projects.
Foreign Aid and Concessional Loans: Official Development Assistance (ODA) received from bilateral partners (such as Japan, USA) and multilateral institutions (such as the World Bank, Asian Development Bank, Islamic Development Bank) in the form of grants and soft loans.
Remittances: Inflows of foreign currency sent home by Bangladeshi expatriates working abroad. This acts as a massive source of liquidity that drives private residential investment, SME capital, and consumption-led economic activities.
External Commercial Borrowing: Loans secured by local businesses or the sovereign government from international commercial capital markets at prevailing market interest rates.
Deficit financing is a financial practice where the government’s total expenditure exceeds its structural revenues (taxes, fees, and profits), and the gap is closed through borrowing from the central bank, commercial banking networks, or public debt instruments. For a developing country like Bangladesh, deficit financing plays a major, deliberate role in catalyzing economic development.
Financing Large-Scale Infrastructure: Developing countries often face severe resource constraints that prevent them from funding vital megaprojects (such as the Padma Bridge, Dhaka Metro Rail, and Deep Sea Ports) solely out of tax revenue. Deficit financing provides the upfront capital necessary to build these high-yielding economic foundations.
Mobilizing Unutilized Resources: In an underdeveloped or developing economy, resources—especially structural surplus labor and unexploited raw materials—frequently sit idle due to lack of demand and capital. Government investment funded by deficits pulls these idle resources into the productive cycle.
Crowding-In Private Investment: Public investments in transport, communication, and energy infrastructure lower the cost of doing business, making private investment projects viable and profitable, thereby inducing additional private capital accumulation.
Mitigating Economic Recessions: During economic downturns or global shocks, private consumption and investment drop. Deficit-financed public spending acts as a counter-cyclical tool to stabilize aggregate demand and maintain momentum.
While deficit financing is highly effective for accelerating development, its utility depends directly on staying within manageable financial boundaries:The Risk of Inflationary Pressures
If deficit financing is channeled toward consumption, unproductive administrative expenses, or long-gestation projects without a near-term increase in consumer goods output, the domestic money supply expands faster than real output. This imbalance causes high structural inflation, which erodes real incomes, worsens income inequality, and destabilizes the macroeconomic environment. Debt Sustainability Constraints: Persistent and unmanaged deficit financing builds up high public debt. If the real GDP growth rate falls below the real cost of debt servicing, an increasing share of national revenue is diverted to pay interest, choking off future developmental expenditures.
Crowding-Out Effect: If the government relies excessively on domestic commercial banks to fund its deficit, it reduces the supply of loanable funds available to the private sector and pushes up interest rates, which can stifle private commercial development.
