4th-semester/bcc-210/bsed-notes.html.description:
To help you prepare quickly, here is a concise composite of the core concepts from Part B, Chapter 1: Concepts and Approaches of Economics of Development, based on past exam questions.
Economic Growth: A narrow, quantitative concept referring to a sustained increase in a country’s real national output or per capita income (GDP/GNI). It focuses on production capacity and macroeconomic indicators.
Economic Development: A broad, multi-dimensional process involving quantitative growth alongside qualitative changes in socio-economic structures. It requires structural shifts (e.g., from agriculture to industry), reduction in poverty, inequality, and unemployment, and improvements in overall human well-being.
Development is measured using composite indices that go beyond monetary metrics:
Human Development Index (HDI): The primary metric measuring health, knowledge, and standard of living.
Other Metrics: Physical Quality of Life Index (PQLI), Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI), and the Gini Coefficient (to measure income inequality).
The HDI evaluates three dimensions using specific indicators:
Health: Measured by Life Expectancy at Birth.
Knowledge: Measured by Mean Years of Schooling (for adults 25+) and Expected Years of Schooling (for children).
Standard of Living: Measured by GNI per capita adjusted for Purchasing Power Parity (PPP).
Methodology: Raw values are normalized into indices between 0 and 1. The final HDI is the Geometric Mean of these three indices ().
MEG (Simon Kuznets): Characterised by high rates of per capita product and population growth, rapid structural transformation (shifting from agriculture to industry/services), and social/ideological modernization.
Determinants of Growth: Economic Factors: Capital formation, natural resources, technological progress, and human capital (labor supply/skills).
Non-Economic Factors: Political stability, rule of law, administrative competence, and favorable social values. Major constraints include the Vicious Circle of Poverty (low income low savings low investment), infrastructure and energy bottlenecks, a narrow export basket (over-reliance on RMG), financial sector instability, skill mismatches in the labor force, and high vulnerability to climate change.Ready to move on to Part B, Chapter 2: Development Theories and Growth Models?Here is the concise composite of the theories and models from Part B, Chapter 2: Development Theories and Growth Models, based on the examination bank.
Core Driver: Development is a spontaneous, discontinuous change driven by Innovation.
The Entrepreneur: A visionary who introduces "new combinations" (new products, methods, markets, sources, or organizations).
Mechanism: Financed by bank credit rather than savings.
Creative Destruction: A cyclical process where successful innovation triggers a boom, followed by a recession where obsolete firms are liquidated to establish a higher equilibrium. Main Thesis: Piecemeal investment fails; a "minimum quantum" of synchronized investment is required to launch an economy out of stagnation.
Three Indivisibilities: Production (SOC): Infrastructure like power/transport requires massive, lump-sum initial capital.
Demand: Complementary industries must start together so workers in one industry can buy goods from another.
Savings: Low incomes result in low savings; a massive push is needed to lift incomes rapidly. Bangladesh Context: Applied through Mega-Projects like the Padma Bridge and Nuclear Power plants to lower transaction costs. Balanced Growth: Simultaneous, synchronized investment across all sectors to create mutual demand.
Unbalanced Growth (Hirschman): Strategic concentration in "leading sectors" with high Linkage Effects. Backward Linkages: Demand for inputs (e.g., garments requiring textiles).
Forward Linkages: Output utilized by others (e.g., steel for construction). Application: Bangladesh follows the Unbalanced Model, prioritizing the RMG sector and Social Overhead Capital (SOC) due to resource scarcity. Core-Periphery: The world is divided into the developed Core and the underdeveloped Periphery.
Unequal Exchange: Periphery nations export low-value raw materials and cheap labor, while the Core exports high-value goods, leading to a net transfer of value to the Core.
Bangladesh: Primarily performs low-value "cut-make-trim" operations in RMG while Core nations capture the high-value design and branding profits.
All societies pass through five stages:
Traditional Society: Subsistence agriculture.
Preconditions for Take-off: Infrastructure and entrepreneurship emerge.
Take-off: Rapid growth in leading manufacturing sectors.
Drive to Maturity: Modern technology spreads across the economy.
High Mass Consumption: Durable goods and services dominate. Bangladesh Status: Passing through Late Take-off and transitioning to Drive to Maturity. Focus: Explains long-run growth via capital accumulation, labor growth, and technological progress.
Steady State: The equilibrium where actual investment () equals "break-even" investment (), stopping changes in capital per worker.
Key Findings: Higher savings increases the level of income but only Technological Progress generates sustained long-run per-capita growth. Harrod-Domar: Growth () is determined by the savings rate () divided by the capital-output ratio (). It highlights the "savings gap" in LDCs.
Nelson’s Low-Level Equilibrium Trap: Stagnation occurs because small income gains are cancelled out by rapid population growth.
Marxian Theory: Class struggle between the Bourgeoisie (owners) and Proletariat (workers). Capitalists accumulate via Surplus Value (unpaid labor), but the falling rate of profit eventually leads to the downfall of capitalism.
Ready to move on to Part B, Chapter 3: Fundamental Issues and Strategies?Here is the composite of the core topics from Part B, Chapter 3: Fundamental Issues and Strategies, focusing on the strategies Bangladesh uses to address structural development challenges.
Sustainable Development: Development that meets present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs. It rests on the "Triple Bottom Line": economic, social, and environmental sustainability.
SDGs (2030 Agenda): A set of 17 interconnected global goals (e.g., No Poverty, Quality Education, Climate Action) designed to end poverty and protect the planet. Bangladesh has localized these into national plans like the Delta Plan 2100. The Inverted-U Curve: Inequality initially rises during early industrialization as labor shifts to high-wage urban sectors and capital concentrates.
The Turning Point: Eventually, inequality falls due to the universalization of education, labor market integration, progressive taxation, and social safety nets. Dimensions: Absolute poverty (lack of basic survival needs) vs. Relative poverty (income inequality relative to the average).
Government SSNPs: Targeted food security (VGD/VGF), employment generation (EGPP), and cash transfers for the elderly or destitute.
NGO Initiatives: Models like BRAC's Ultra-Poor Graduation (asset transfer + training) and Grameen Bank's Microcredit (collateral-free loans for self-employment) are globally recognized for empowering the poor and women. Vision 2021: Aimed to transform Bangladesh into a middle-income, "Digital Bangladesh" by its 50th anniversary through growth and ICT modernization.
8th Five-Year Plan (FY2021–2025): Focuses on "Promoting Prosperity and Fostering Inclusiveness". It targets 8%–8.5% GDP growth, poverty reduction to 15.6%, and preparedness for LDC graduation by diversifying exports. The Trap: Low income Low savings/demand Low investment Low productivity Low income.
The Solution: Requires a "Big Push" of coordinated, large-scale investment in Social Overhead Capital (SOC) to jumpstart demand and production. Challenges: LDCs face high costs, technological dependency, and "inappropriate" capital-intensive bias from developed nations that can worsen unemployment.
Appropriate Package: For Bangladesh, this must be labor-intensive, adaptable "intermediate" technology that is "unbundled" (avoiding turnkey contracts) to ensure local skill-building and maintenance capacity. Definition: Sustainable use of ocean resources for growth and jobs while preserving ecosystem health.
Potential: Following the resolution of maritime disputes, Bangladesh can leverage its 118,813 sq km of sea for deep-sea fishing, seaweed culture, shipbuilding, and eco-tourism to enhance food security and poverty alleviation. Planning Systems: Bangladesh uses a Mixed Economy (composite) model, combining imperative planning for the public sector with indicative policies for the private sector.
BOP Structure: Tracks transactions via the Current Account (Trade, Services, and Remittances), Capital Account, and Financial Account (FDI and Loans).
Ready to move on to Part B, Chapter 4: Local and International Measures?Here is the concise composite for Part B, Chapter 4: Local and International Measures for Economic Development, focusing on strategies and external factors.
Role: Acts as the economy's backbone, contributing 20–25% of GDP and nearly 80–89% of industrial employment.
Benefits: High labor intensity, low capital requirements, poverty alleviation, and utilization of local raw materials like jute, cane, and leather.
Main Industries: Handloom (Jamdani), pottery, bamboo/cane crafts, and food processing. Foreign Aid Drawbacks: While it fills the savings gap, it creates a "Dependency Syndrome," leading to a "Debt Trap" (loans over grants) and "Tied Aid" (mandating purchases from donor nations at inflated prices).
Capital Formation: The process of adding to physical capital (machinery, infrastructure). Domestic Sources: Voluntary savings, taxation, and public enterprise profits.
Foreign Sources: FDI, remittances, and concessional loans. Role: A deliberate strategy where spending exceeds revenue to fund mega-projects (e.g., Padma Bridge) and mobilize idle resources.
Risks: Can cause "Demand-Pull Inflation" if the money supply outpaces real output, and a "Crowding-Out Effect" where government borrowing depletes credit for the private sector.
Safe Limit: Generally maintained at or below 5% of GDP to preserve macroeconomic stability. Assistance Debate: Positive: MNCs bring FDI, "Technology Spillovers" (modern know-how), and integrate the host country into global value chains.
Negative: "Profit Repatriation" (sending earnings back to home countries), "Crowding-Out" local infant industries, and potential exploitation of cheap labor or lax environmental laws. Free Trade: Rooted in "Comparative Advantage" (specializing in low-opportunity-cost goods).
Critique: The "Prebisch-Singer Hypothesis" warns that the terms of trade for primary/low-value exports (like RMG) deteriorate over time against manufactured goods.
Integration: Involves reducing trade barriers (e.g., SAFTA). Benefits include "Economies of Scale" and "Trade Creation," but risks include "Trade Diversion" (shifting trade to a higher-cost member nation). Fiscal Policy: Uses the Annual Development Program (ADP) for infrastructure and Social Safety Net Programs (SSNPs) for equity.
Monetary Policy: Managed by Bangladesh Bank to control inflation and exchange rates using tools like the Repo Rate, Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR), and Statutory Liquidity Ratio (SLR). Stages: Moves from high birth/death rates to low birth/death rates.
Bangladesh's Status: Firmly in Stage 3 (Late Expanding), characterized by falling birth rates and low death rates.
Demographic Dividend: A window where the working-age population (15–64) outnumbers dependents, offering a prime opportunity for growth.
Ready to conclude with Part B, Chapter 5: Comparative Analysis and Lessons for Bangladesh?To wrap up your preparation, here is the concise composite for Part B, Chapter 5: Comparative Analysis and Lessons for Bangladesh, which synthesises the structural differences between nations and the roadmap for Bangladesh's graduation.A country is classified as developed when it exhibits high economic performance and an advanced quality of life. Key determinants include:
Income & Wealth: A sustained High Gross National Income (GNI) per capita that meets high-income thresholds.
Human Development: A High Human Development Index (HDI) score (typically >0.800), driven by near-universal literacy and premium healthcare access.
Economic Structure: An economy dominated by tertiary (services) and quaternary (knowledge/research) sectors with high levels of automation.
Institutional Maturity: Robust rule of law, political stability, and minimal corruption.
To transition successfully past the middle-income threshold, Bangladesh must adopt several core strategies used by advanced economies:
Prioritise Human Capital: Shift the national focus from providing "cheap labor" to developing a highly skilled, technical, and STEM-focused workforce.
Industrial Diversification: Reduce the "concentration risk" of over-reliance on the RMG sector by aggressively incentivizing pharmaceuticals, precision electronics, and IT services.
Institutional Strengthening: Improve the "Ease of Doing Business" by ensuring judicial efficiency in contract enforcement and streamlining bureaucratic processes.
Foster Native Innovation: Build structured links between academic research and the private sector to promote localized industrial patents rather than just adopting foreign hardware.
Infrastructure Modernization: Scale up Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs) to build smart grids and deep-sea ports to lower transaction costs.
We have now covered every chapter in Part B. Since your exam is in about 20 minutes, would you like me to quickly generate a set of Flashcards or a Practice Quiz to test your memory on these key terms?Here is the concise composite of core concepts from Part A, Chapter 2: Physical and Natural Resources of Bangladesh, based on past exam questions.Bangladesh possesses significant non-renewable resources essential for its industrial and energy sectors:
Natural Gas: The primary commercial fuel, powering over half of national electricity and serving as the vital raw material for urea fertilizer.
Coal: High-grade bituminous coal used for thermal power (e.g., Barapukuria) and brick manufacturing.
Hard Rock (Granite): Extracted from Maddhapara for mega-infrastructure, railway ballast, and flood-control embankments.
Limestone: A foundational input for the domestic cement and lime industries.
Others: Silica sand (glass), white clay (ceramics), and mineral sands (heavy minerals like zircon).
Optimal utilization of these resources acts as a developmental catalyst by:
Energy Security: Reducing reliance on expensive imported LNG and petroleum.
Foreign Exchange Savings: Replacing imports with domestic inputs to stabilize the Balance of Payments.
Agricultural Productivity: Providing gas for domestic fertilizer production, which ensures national food security. Features: Extremely high density (>1,200 people/sq km), rapid urbanization, and a "Demographic Dividend" where the working-age population (15–64) outnumbers dependents.
Economic Impact of Overpopulation: High density puts severe pressure on arable land (conversion to housing), stretches public utilities (health/education) beyond capacity, and leads to structural unemployment.
Rapid growth has created significant ecological trade-offs:
Pollution: Discharge of untreated industrial effluents has turned rivers like the Buriganga into "dead zones".
Resource Depletion: Intensive irrigation and industrial washing (textiles) have caused alarming drops in groundwater tables.
Degradation: Unregulated brick kilns strip fertile topsoil and worsen urban air quality.
Following the resolution of maritime disputes with India and Myanmar, Bangladesh acquired 118,813 sq km of maritime territory. This "Blue Economy" offers massive potential for deep-sea fishing, maritime transport, shipbuilding, and eco-tourism.Ready to move on to Part A, Chapter 3: Political, Administrative and Legal Structure?Here is the composite of the core concepts regarding the Political, Administrative and Legal Structure of Bangladesh, focusing on the 1972 Constitution and the mechanics of the state.The Constitution is the supreme law of the land, adopted on November 4, 1972. The original document is highly progressive and characterized by:
Four Fundamental Principles: Article 8 anchors the state on Nationalism (Bengali culture/identity), Socialism (economic justice), Democracy (human rights/participation), and Secularism (elimination of communalism and religious discrimination).
Structure of Government: It established a Parliamentary (Westminster-style) system where the Prime Minister holds real executive power and is accountable to the legislature.
Unitary State: Unlike a federal system, all administrative and legislative power flows from a single central government in Dhaka.
Key Legal Protections: It provides for an Independent Judiciary (Supreme Court) and guarantees Enforceable Fundamental Rights, allowing citizens to move the court if their rights are violated.
The Jatiya Sangsad is the unicameral (single-chamber) supreme lawmaking organ of the state.
Composition: It consists of 350 seats. 300 members are directly elected from territorial constituencies.
50 seats are exclusively reserved for women, allocated proportionally to political parties based on their share of elected seats. Powers: Its primary functions include enacting laws, exercising "power of the purse" (no tax or expenditure can occur without its approval), and holding the executive cabinet accountable through standing committees.
The current administrative system is often critiqued for its colonial-bureaucratic roots. Key proposed reforms for efficiency include:
Digitalization (Smart Governance): Transitioning to paperless, AI-assisted workflows to reduce "red tape" and institutional corruption.
Merit-Based System: Shifting from seniority-based promotions to a strict Performance Appraisal System (PAS) to ensure competent leadership.
Decentralization: Transferring fiscal and planning autonomy from the central secretariats to local government tiers (Upazila and Zila Parishads) to better address grassroots needs.
Accountability: Strengthening oversight bodies like the Anti-Corruption Commission (ACC) and implementing the Office of the Ombudsman as mandated by Article 77 of the Constitution.
Ready to move on to Part A, Chapter 4: Socio-culture of Bangladesh?Here is the concise composite for Part A, Chapter 4: Socio-culture of Bangladesh, focusing on social structures, cultural challenges, and migration patterns.The socio-economic landscape is challenged by several systemic issues:
Overpopulation and Density: Extreme pressure on limited land, public healthcare, and educational infrastructure.
Poverty and Inequality: Despite progress, many remain vulnerable to economic shocks, and a widening wealth gap threatens social cohesion.
Gender Vulnerabilities: Systemic barriers include wage disparity, restricted leadership access, and persistent issues like child marriage and dowry.
Institutional Quality: Public education often fails to meet market demands, leading to "educated unemployment" due to skill mismatches.
Healthcare Access: High out-of-pocket expenses frequently drive low-income families back into poverty during medical emergencies. Superstition: Entrenched dogmatic beliefs in marginalized pockets sometimes impede scientific and medical progression.
Cultural Aggression: Rapid influx of global digital media can create identity conflicts or generational gaps among youth.
Erosion of Value Systems: Urbanization is breaking down traditional rural joint family structures, leaving the elderly without communal safety nets.
Moral Decay: Increased commercialization is linked to rising drug dependency and systemic corruption, eroding civic trust.
This demographic shift is driven by structural imbalances between villages and cities:
The "Push" (Rural Realities): Natural disasters (riverbank erosion, salinity), chronic landlessness, and a lack of quality social infrastructure expel people from villages.
The "Pull" (Urban Attractions): Concentration of industries (RMG), higher and more reliable daily wages, and better access to civic amenities attract migrants.
Consequences: Positive: Provides a steady supply of low-cost labor for industry and generates internal remittance flows that stabilize rural consumption.
Negative: Leads to overcrowded urban slums (bastees), severe strain on municipal transport and drainage, and an agricultural "brain drain" as active workers leave villages. That concludes Part A. We have finished all requested chapters! Since your exam is in 10 minutes, would you like a quick "Lightning Round" quiz on the most frequently asked terms, or perhaps a tailored report summarizing the most important "Mega-Projects" and "Theories" to skim one last time?

(b) Explain the effects and safe limits of deficit financing on economic development in Bangladesh. [7]
(a) What makes a country "developed"? What are the barriers to development for developing countries? [07]
(b) Give a comparison between developed and developing counties. What lessons do you learn from developed countries for Bangladesh? [04]
(a) What makes a country developed? Compare the features of developed and developing countries. [5]
(c) Give a comparison between developed and developing countries. What lessons do you learn from developed countries for Bangladesh? [05]

4th-semester/4th-semester-final-exam-202.icon: <svg class="svg-icon lucide-file" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M6 22a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h8a2.4 2.4 0 0 1 1.704.706l3.588 3.588A2.4 2.4 0 0 1 20 8v12a2 2 0 0 1-2 2z"></path><path d="M14 2v5a1 1 0 0 0 1 1h5"></path></svg>
4th-semester/4th-semester-final-exam-202.description:
(a) Construct your own definition of 'risk' and justify the conceptual boundary between risk and uncertainty. (b) Justify the necessity of risk management in contemporary business operations. Identify at least four common organisational risks and outline the sequential stages of an effective risk management framework. (c) Contrast pure risk with speculative risk using real-life scenarios. Show how the element of loss operates differently in each category. For each of the following risk situations, design an appropriate risk management strategy (single or combination) and defend your choice:
(a) Rahim, a 45-year-old bank manager, has a sedentary lifestyle and his father died of a heart attack at age 55. (b) A private investment fund notices unusual volatility in the stock market that threatens its portfolio value. (c) A software development firm discovers critical security loopholes in its client database system. (d) A small business owner fears bankruptcy because of mounting personal loan obligations. (e) A garment export company has three factories located in coastal areas frequently hit by cyclones and tidal surges. (a) Explain the interrelationship between loss frequency and loss severity in risk assessment. Use examples to show how a change in one can impact the other. (b) How can quantitative methods like probability distributions and statistical analysis be used to assess loss frequency and severity? Illustrate with an example. (c) Discuss how changes in economic conditions such as inflation and interest rates affect the demand for insurance among individuals and organisations. (a) Distinguish between insurance and gambling. Illustrate your answer with suitable examples, and explain why insurance is generally regarded as a socially beneficial practice, whereas gambling is not. (b) Explain the principle of utmost good faith in insurance contracts. Why is this principle essential for maintaining trust and fairness between the insurer and the insured? (c) Discuss the significance of the principle of "insurable interest" in insurance contracts. Illustrate your answer with suitable examples. (d) Discuss the purpose and importance of reinsurance in the insurance industry. Explain how reinsurance enables insurance companies to manage their risk exposure effectively. (a) Craft a precise definition of life insurance and highlight its primary mission. Show how life insurance serves as a tool for intergenerational financial security. (b) Contrast term life, whole life, and universal life insurance on parameters such as premium structure, cash value, and suitability for different life stages. (c) Identify the pressing challenges confronting the life insurance industry today and examine recent trends including insurtech innovations and evolving consumer expectations. (a) Explain the concept of marine insurance and discuss its significance in international trade. Illustrate your answer with suitable examples. (b) Discuss the key elements of a marine insurance policy. Define the following types of marine insurance policies: i. Time Policy
ii. Unvalued Policy
iii. Floating Policy
iv. Blanket Policy
v. Block Policy (a) Enumerate and briefly characterise the diverse life insurance product categories currently operated and administered by Jiban Bima Corporation. (b) Compose a concise analytical note on the establishment, mandate, and key functions of the Insurance Development and Regulatory Authority (IDRA) of Bangladesh. (c) Propose a set of actionable policy measures and strategic initiatives that the Government of Bangladesh ought to adopt in order to enhance the public appeal and penetration of the insurance business. (a) Define ethics. Identify the relationship between ethics and law. (b) Briefly explain the importance of business ethics. (c) Point out the relevance of globalisation for business ethics. (a) Explain the key components of an ethical decision in business. How do individual factors, such as moral development, personal values, and cognitive biases, influence ethical decision-making? (b) Define Sustainability and explain how the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) framework shifts the goal of a corporation from purely economic value to an extended set of social and environmental goals. (c) A Bangladeshi tea estate owner finds that using a specific pesticide increases yield by 30% but is contaminating into local water sources used by villagers. Using the TBL framework, argue why the owner should or should not continue using the pesticide. Consider the economic benefits, social impacts, and environmental consequences in your answer. (a) Discuss the ethical issues that arise in the firm-employee relationship. How does globalisation create additional ethical challenges for companies employing people in developing countries? (b) Outline the core rights and duties of employees in an organisation. How does workplace discrimination violate these rights? (c) FoodGiant is a large food and beverage company. In developing countries, it advertises sugary foods and drinks to children using cartoon characters, free toys, and school programmes. However, in developed countries, the company promotes its own products as healthy choices. At the same time, workers in FoodGiant's factories work 12 hours a day without extra pay for overtime. Employees who try to form a labour union often lose their jobs. Required: i. Identify the ethical issues related to FoodGiant's treatment of consumers and employees.
ii. Explain why the company's marketing practices in developing countries can be considered a double standard.
iii. Discuss whether FoodGiant's actions are socially responsible. Support your answer with reasons. (a) Define business ethics management. Explain its components. (b) Briefly explain the different types of ethical codes. (c) Identify the reasons for social accounting by the organisations. (a) How does the government function as a stakeholder in business ethics? What ethical issues typically arise in the relationship between business and government, particularly in developing economies? (b) What is meant by 'corporate citizenship'? Discuss the relationship between viewing a business as a "key player in the regulatory game" versus a "passive rule-follower". How should corporations engage with government in an ethical manner? (c) MegaPort Ltd. is a large port infrastructure company seeking a major government contract in a South Asian country. Company representatives paid government officials a fee to accelerate the approval process, although illegal under international anti-bribery laws. This is a common local practice. The company argues that without such payments, it cannot compete with the local firms who routinely engage in such practices. Also, it claims that the project will bring thousands of jobs and local people. Required: Analyze the ethical dimensions of MegaPort Ltd.'s conduct using the concepts of government as stakeholder, corporate citizenship, and globalisation of business-government relations. Is the 'common local practice' argument ethically valid? What systemic changes in both corporate governance and regulatory frameworks would help prevent such ethical violations? (a) Explain the Agency Relationship between shareholders (principals) and managers (agents), identifying the roles of conflict of interest and informational asymmetry. (b) A large multinational company seeks a hostile takeover of a traditional Bangladeshi food brand without the board's approval. Evaluate the potential impact of this takeover on employee welfare and local community identity. (c) In a major Bangladeshi pharmaceutical firm, the CEO sold a significant volume of personal shares in the company one week before the public announcement of a failed drug trial that caused the stock price to fall by 35%. The information about the drug trial failure was known internally months before the announcement. Minority shareholders suffered major losses. Regulators launched an investigation, but the CEO argued the sale was 'planned in advance' for personal financial reasons. Required: Analyze the above case from the perspectives of (i) ethical issues in corporate governance, (ii) shareholder rights. What governance reforms should the company implement to prevent such a situation? (a) Distinguish between Consequentialist and Non-consequentialist ethical theories. Provide one business example for each to illustrate their application. (b) Compare and contrast egoism, Utilitarianism, and ethics of duties. Which theory do you consider most applicable to modern business decision-making and why? (c) Nadia is the operations director of a garment factory in Chittagong. Year-end profits are strong. She faces a choice: (1) distribute a substantial bonus to all 500 workers, improving their living standards but reducing investor dividends; or (2) pay higher dividends to shareholders to retain investment for planned expansion, which may create 200 new jobs next year but provides nothing to current workers now. Some senior managers suggest a third path: allocate funds to improve safety equipment, which satisfies no one immediately but reduces accident risk in future periods. Required: Apply at least two ethical theories to evaluate each of Nadia's three options. Which course of action is most ethically defensible? Justify your reasoning. (a) Passion works as a driving force for a successful entrepreneur. Elucidate the statement by defining Entrepreneur and Entrepreneurship. (b) Define execution intelligence. How would you relate it with an entrepreneur? (c) Mention some attributes of firms that pursue high levels of corporate entrepreneurship. (d) In 1978, Ayesha Abed helped establish Aarong in Bangladesh to create sustainable income opportunities for rural artisans, especially women. At that time, many artisans struggled to find reliable markets for their handcrafted products and often faced delayed payments. Ayesha Abed recognized this problem and transformed it into an entrepreneurial opportunity by connecting rural producers with urban consumers through Aarong. Her success demonstrates several key entrepreneurial qualities. First, she showed vision by identifying an unmet market need. Second, she displayed leadership by organising thousands of artisans into a productive network. Third, she exhibited social responsibility, ensuring fair prices and employment opportunities for disadvantaged women. Finally, her perseverance and innovation helped Aarong grow from a small outlet into one of Bangladesh's leading lifestyle retail brands. Today, Aarong supports tens of thousands of artisans and serves as a successful example of how entrepreneurial qualities can create both business success and positive social impact in Bangladesh. Required: i) Which entrepreneurial quality was most important in establishing Aarong? Why? ii) How did leadership contribute to Aarong's success? iii) In what way did Aarong create social value in Bangladesh? (a) How can finding a gap in the marketplace create a business opportunity for an entrepreneur to pursue? (b) How does creativity contribute to the opportunity recognition process? (c) Point out some traits that help an entrepreneur in successful opportunity recognition. (d) In many areas of coastal Bangladesh, especially in districts such as Satkhira and Khulna, people face severe shortages of safe drinking water due to salinity in groundwater. During the dry season, families often travel long distances to collect fresh water, which consumes time and affects health and productivity. Recognising this problem, Drinkwell introduced community-based water purification systems that convert saline and contaminated water into safe drinking water. The company installs purification plants in villages and operates them through local entrepreneurs. Residents can purchase clean water at an affordable price while local operators earn income from managing the facilities. This solution addressed a significant social problem while creating a sustainable business opportunity. As a result, thousands of people gained access to safe drinking water, waterborne diseases were reduced, and local employment opportunities were created. The case demonstrates how identifying a community problem can lead to innovative entrepreneurial ventures that generate both social and economic value. Required: i) What problem did Drinkwell identify in coastal Bangladesh? ii) Why can this case be considered an entrepreneurial opportunity? iii) What innovation did the company introduce? (a) What is the best time for a firm to develop its business model? (b) Pathao, one of Bangladesh's leading digital platforms, emerged in 2015 as a motorcycle-based ride-sharing service at a time when Dhaka's traffic congestion was among the worst in the world. Traditional transportation—taxis, rickshaws, and buses—struggled to offer reliability or speed. Pathao introduced a disruptive business model built on smartphone connectivity, real-time matching, and a gig-based rider network. Instead of owning vehicles, Pathao empowered thousands of freelance bikers to join the platform, drastically lowering operational costs and enabling rapid scale-up. Pathao further expanded into food delivery, courier services, and digital payments through Pathao Pay, creating a multi-sided digital ecosystem. Each service disrupted a separate traditional sector: restaurant deliveries, parcel logistics, and even cash-based transactions. Its pricing transparency, quick service, and user-friendly app attracted a large urban young population, while conventional transport operators and courier companies faced declining demand and competitive pressure. By leveraging local market insights—such as acceptance of mobile payments and the widespread use of affordable smartphones—Pathao adapted global ride-sharing concepts to Bangladeshi realities. The company's disruptive approach not only redefined urban mobility but also generated income opportunities for tens of thousands of riders and delivery agents, reshaping Bangladesh's digital economy. Required: i) What made Pathao's business model disruptive in Bangladesh? ii) Which traditional industries were most affected by Pathao's expansion? iii) What local factors enabled Pathao's success in the Bangladeshi market? (c) Briefly point out the main contents of a business plan. (d) In 2022, three graduates from Chattogram noticed that many farmers in rural Bangladesh struggled to sell fresh vegetables at fair prices because of multiple intermediaries. At the same time, urban consumers often paid high prices for vegetables that were not always fresh. Seeing this gap, the entrepreneurs developed a business plan for Aamra Fresh Agro, a startup that would connect farmers directly with consumers through a mobile app and delivery network. The business plan included a market analysis showing increasing demand for safe and fresh food in major cities such as Dhaka and Chattogram. The founders estimated an initial investment of BDT 15 lakh for app development, cold-storage facilities, and delivery vehicles. Revenue would come from a commission on each sale and subscription packages for regular customers. The plan identified potential risks, including transportation delays, seasonal fluctuations in production, and competition from established online grocery platforms. To address these challenges, the entrepreneurs planned partnerships with local farmer cooperatives and logistics providers. They also projected that the business would break even within three years if monthly sales grew by 10%. After presenting the business plan to investors, the startup secured seed funding and launched operations. Within the first year, it supplied fresh vegetables to more than 2,000 households and increased participating farmers' incomes by approximately 20%. Required: i) What were the main sources of revenue for Aamra Fresh Agro? ii) What risks were identified in the business plan? (a) What are the prerequisites for building a strong ethical culture in a firm? (b) Define the concept liability of newness. What can a new venture do to overcome the liability of newness? (c) Why does having relevant industry experience help the founder of a firm to be successful? (d) FreshBrew is a mobile coffee truck business started by college friends Mia and Arif. They noticed many commuters near their city's business district lacked quick access to quality coffee. After securing a small investor, they launched the truck and hired an assistant manager. Although they had a great product, the business struggled. Customers sometimes waited too long, and social media feedback was slow. Mia realised that they needed more help with operations and marketing to grow. They also felt inexperienced running a business and had no credible reputation with suppliers yet. Mia and Arif invited two industry advisors with café experience to join their team to help with training and supplier contracts. Required: i. What concept explains FreshBrew's early struggles because the founders lacked experience and reputation? ii. Why might adding industry advisors help FreshBrew? iii. What is the benefit of having more than one founder or team member? (a) Define acquisition with an example. Why do acquisitions fail sometimes? (b) What challenges do second-generation entrepreneurs face when taking over established family business? (c) Amena Akter, a resident of Cumilla, started a small online clothing business in 2019. She sold traditional Bangladeshi dresses through social media and initially operated from her home with a small amount of personal savings. Her products gained popularity among local customers, and she planned to expand her business by opening a boutique and hiring employees. However, Amena faced several challenges common to many women entrepreneurs in Bangladesh. One major challenge was limited access to finance. Banks often required collateral and lengthy documentation, which made it difficult for her to obtain a business loan. She also encountered social barriers, as some family and community members believed that women should prioritise household responsibilities over business activities. Another challenge was balancing family and business commitments. As a mother of two children, Amena had to manage household duties while handling customer orders, supplier negotiations, and marketing activities. Additionally, she faced difficulties in building professional networks, which are often crucial for obtaining business information and opportunities. The COVID-19 pandemic further disrupted her business by reducing customer demand and creating supply-chain problems. Despite these obstacles, Amena adapted by increasing her online marketing efforts, offering home delivery services, and participating in entrepreneurship training programmes organised by local support organisations. Today, her business employs six women and serves customers across Bangladesh. Amena's story demonstrates both the significant challenges faced by women entrepreneurs and their ability to overcome barriers through resilience, innovation, and determination. Required: i) What financial challenge did Amena face? ii) What social barrier affected her business growth? iii) How did family responsibilities impact her entrepreneurship? iv) What strategies helped her overcome challenges? (a) Identify the keys to effective new product development. Explain the common reasons new products fail. (b) Explain the difference between internal growth strategies and external growth strategies. (c) Explain "licensing" and how it can be used as a growth strategy. (a) Explain why most new ventures need to raise money at some point during their early life? (b) Identify the three steps involved in properly preparing to raise debt or equity financing. (c) Explain why an initial public offering is an important milestone for a firm. (d) Explain the advantages of leasing for an entrepreneurial firm. (a) Define organisational behaviour. What is the importance of interpersonal skills in the workplace? (b) Describe the major behavioural science disciplines that contribute to Organisational Behaviour. (c) Why might the same Organisational Behaviour concept succeed in one organisation but fail in another? Analyze using any two challenges and two opportunities managers face in applying Organisational Behaviour concepts. (a) A software company needs to hire for a programming role, while a construction company needs to hire for a heavy equipment operator role. Analyse which type of ability (intellectual or physical) is more critical for each role and why. (b) What are biographical characteristics? Identify and explain how they affect Organisational Behaviour. (c) What causes job satisfaction? Compare the relative importance of pay versus the work itself. (a) What are the three components of attitude? How can managers use an understanding of attitudes to predict employee behaviour? (b) What is Emotional Labour? Distinguish between surface acting and deep acting with examples from a customer service job. (c) GreenMart Superstore employs a large team of cashiers and customer service representatives. In the past six months, management noticed rising absenteeism, customer complaints, and employee turnover. Exit interviews revealed employees felt unappreciated, emotionally exhausted, and dissatisfied with their work. Several employees stated they had to 'fake smiles' all day despite dealing with rude customers and heavy workloads. Required: i. Which major job attitudes—job satisfaction, organisational commitment, or employee engagement—seem most affected at GreenMart? Explain with reference to the case.
ii. How can the concept of Emotional Intelligence (EI) help GreenMart's supervisors reduce employee burnout and improve morale? (a) Briefly describe the Big Five Personality Model. Which trait do you think is most important for a manager in Bangladesh's business environment? Justify your answer. (b) What is perception? Explain any three common perceptual shortcuts (biases) that managers should be aware of when evaluating employees. (c) What is the link between perception and individual decision making? How can organisational constraints affect decisions? (a) What is a group? Explain the stages of group development in your own words. (b) What is the difference between a work group and a work team? What are the key characteristics of an effective team? (c) BDCom Networks formed a cross-functional project team to develop a new billing system. The team consists of members from IT, Finance, and Customer Service departments. In the first few weeks, members were polite but uncertain about roles. By the second month, open conflicts arose over who should lead. Members from different departments argued about priorities, and the project fell behind schedule. Required: i. At which stage of group development is this team currently? Justify your answer using evidence from the case.
ii. As the team leader, suggest two specific actions you would take to move the team to the 'Norming' or 'Performing' stage. (a) Why is leadership important in organisations? Briefly distinguish between charismatic leadership and transformational leadership. (b) Define power. What are the five bases of power explored in the text? Which base of power is most sustainable in the long run, and why? (c) Mr. X was recently promoted to Factory Manager at DynaFab Ltd. He noticed that several senior workers ignored his instructions but quickly obeyed orders from the previous manager, who had worked at DynaFab for 20 years. Mr. X relies mostly on his formal authority (position) to gain compliance, but the results have been poor. Workers seem to respect only those they consider experienced and trustworthy. Required: i. Which type of power is Mr. X relying on, and which power bases does the previous manager likely possess? Use the case to support your answer.
ii. What advice would you give Mr. X to build a more effective power base and gain genuine influence over his team? (a) Define conflict. Explain the five stages of the conflict process as described in the textbook. (b) What is organisational change? What are the most common reasons employees resist change? Suggest two ways managers can overcome resistance. (c) MegaBank recently merged with a smaller bank. Following the merger, employees experienced role ambiguity, fear of job loss, increased workloads, and poor communication from management. Many employees began reporting headaches, insomnia, and reduced concentration. Absenteeism rose by 30%. A group of senior tellers refused to adopt the new software system, causing operational delays. Required: i. Identify at least two sources of stress (organisational and personal) that MegaBank employees are experiencing. What consequences of stress are visible in this case?
ii. As an HR consultant, recommend one individual-level and one organisational-level strategy to help MegaBank manage stress effectively during this change. (a) Write a brief on the history of Bangladesh mentioning Pakistan movement and the birth of Pakistan, language movement (1948-1952), Juktafront election of 1954, six points programme of 1966, mass upsurge of 1969, election of 1970 and birth of Bangladesh in 1971. (b) What do you know about the constitution of Bangladesh? Discuss the features of constitution-1972 of people's republic of Bangladesh. (c) Discuss the judiciary system of Bangladesh. (a) Discuss the characteristics of Bangladesh economy. What are the reasons for underdevelopment of Bangladesh? (b) Mention the main cottage industries in Bangladesh. What role small and cottage industries can play for a developing country like Bangladesh? (c) What are the features of the population of Bangladesh? Discuss the impact of overpopulation on the Bangladesh economy. (a) Is economic development means economic growth? How can you measure the Human Development Index (HDI)? Briefly discuss the factors that determine economic growth. (b) Explain the Kuznets hypothesis of economic growth and income distribution. What are the causes of increase in income inequality with economic development? (c) What do you mean by sustainable development? Briefly discuss the sustainable development goals. (a) What is poverty? Discuss the poverty alleviation programme of different organisations in Bangladesh. (b) Write a brief on the 8th five-year plan of Bangladesh. (c) What do you mean by micro credit? Do you think that microcredit programme can reduce poverty in Bangladesh? Discuss the major activities of NGOs. (a) What drives economic development according to Schumpeter? Discuss the salient features of Schumpeter model of economic development. (b) What is the big push theory? Why is big push needed in a less developed country? (c) Discuss the theories of balanced and unbalanced growth. Which model do you support to be applicable for developing countries like Bangladesh? (a) How capital formation can be a measure of economic development of Bangladesh? Discuss the sources of capital formation. (b) Discuss the theory of demographic transition in economic development with criticism. Bangladesh in what part of this theory? (c) "A strong agricultural economy brings social progress" – Do you agree? Explain your view. (a) Give a comparison between developed and developing countries. What lessons do you learn from developed countries for Bangladesh? (b) Discuss the traditional theory of free trade with regard to its effects on a country's development. (c) What measures encourage economic integration among developing countries? Discuss the benefits from and problems of economic integration.

4th-semester/syllabus-4th-semester.html.description:
Marks: 100
Credit: 3 hours
(Written: 75, Internal Evaluation: 20, Viva: 05)Insurance and Risk Management is a core course of business administration discipline of University of Chittagong. It is designed to provide an introduction to the basic principles of risk and insurance prevalent in financial decision making.
To acquaint students with the basic knowledge of the principles of risk and insurance and the methods of risk management found in strategic planning.
To provide students with a basic knowledge of general, life and health insurance.
To help students develop a sound personal insurance program for meeting the major risks in our society.
To give students some basic principles of insurance buying, with an emphasis on how to develop a strong insurance and financial estate planning program.
To offer a systematic framework for analysing any insurance contract.
To learn about the ethical issues facing business and an appreciation of the framework for making ethical decisions. Introduction to Risk Management: Concept, Types of Risk, Risk Faced by Businesses and Individuals; Risk Management, The Risk Management Process and Methods, and Business Risk Management Organisation, Factors affecting risk.
Objective of Risk Management: The Need for a Risk Management Objective, Understanding the Costs of Risk, Firm Value Maximisation and the Cost of Risk, Individual Risk Management and the Cost of Risk, and Risk Management & Societal Welfare.
Risk Identification and Measurement: Risk Identification; Basic Concepts from Probability and Statistics; and Evaluating the Frequency & Severity of Losses.
Risk Management by Individuals and Corporations: Risk Aversion and Demand for Insurance by Individuals - the Effects of Insurance on Wealth, Risk Aversion, Other Factors Affecting an Individual's Demand for Insurance; and Business Risk Management - Shareholder Diversification - Closely Held Business - Why purchase insurance when shareholders are diversified.
Introduction to Insurance: Definition and Basic Characteristics of Insurance, Requirement of an Insurable Risk, Adverse Selection and Insurance, Insurance and Gambling Compared, Insurance and Hedging Compared, Types of Insurance, Benefits and Costs of Insurance to Society.
Life Insurance: Life Insurance Product Overview, Traditional Products: Term, Endowment, and Whole Life Insurance, Product Innovation: Universal and Variable Life Insurance, Tax benefits from Life Insurance Policies, Annuity Contracts, Life Insurance Pricing, How much Life Insurance should be covered, and Life Insurance Cost Comparisons.
Fire Insurance: Meaning - Principles, Elements of Contract - Classification of Fire Insurance Policies - Policy Conditions - Determination of Premium - Fire Claims.
Marine Insurance: Meaning - Subject Matter - Importance - Marine Insurance Contract - Types of Marine Policies - Policy conditions - Marine Losses - Claims and their Settlement.
Other Insurance: Health, Automobile, Social Insurance, and other Miscellaneous Insurance Policies.
Insurance Practices in Bangladesh: Insurance Acts and Regulations of Bangladesh, Organization and Functions of Jiban Bima Corporation and Sadharan Bima Corporation, Role of Private Insurance Companies, and Problems & Prospects of Insurance Business in Bangladesh. Harrington Niehaus – Risk Management & Insurance (2nd Edition)
George E. Rejda – Principles of Risk Management and Insurance (10th Edition)
M. N. Mishra – Insurance Principles and Practices (22nd Edition) Azizul Huq Chaudhuri – Risk and Insurance (2nd Edition)
Shahiduddin Ahmed – Insurance Business in Bangladesh: A Study of the Pattern, Problems & Prospects
Publications of Insurance Development and Regulatory Authority (IDRA)
Publications of Government of Bangladesh regarding laws relevant to insurance, rules & regulations issued from time to time.
Publications of Jiban Bima Corporation & Sadharan Bima Corporation.
Marks: 100
Credit: 3 hours
(Written: 75, Internal Evaluation: 20, Viva: 05)The course aims at understanding business ethics, its application, and management. To achieve the objectives, the course will highlight the theories of business ethics. The course will also elaborately explain the roles and responsibilities as well as rights and privileges of different stakeholders in the context of business ethics.
Introduction to Business Ethics: Definition, Importance, Globalization – a key context of business ethics? Sustainability – a key goal for business ethics?
Theories of Business Ethics: Normative ethical theories – Individual vs. Institutional morality, Questioning vs. accepting capitalism, Justifying vs. applying moral norms, Traditional ethical theories – Consequentialist Theories: Egoism, Utilitarianism, Non-consequentialist Theories: Ethics of duties, Ethics of rights and justice, Limits of Traditional Theories, Contemporary ethical theories – Virtue ethics, Feminist ethics, Discourse ethics, Postmodern ethics.
Making Decisions in Business Ethics: Ethical decision, Models of ethical decision-making, Individual influence on ethical decision-making, Situational influence on decision-making.
Managing Business Ethics: Business ethics management, Setting standards of ethical behavior: designing and implementing codes of ethics, Managing stakeholder relations, Assessing ethical performance, Organizing for business ethics management.
Shareholders and Business Ethics: Introduction: reassessing the importance of shareholders as stakeholders, Shareholders as stakeholders: understanding corporate governance, Ethical issues in corporate governance: Executive accountability and control, Executive remuneration, Ethical aspects of mergers and acquisitions, The role of financial markets and insider trading, The role of accountants, Reforming governance.
Employees and Business Ethics: Employees as stakeholders, Ethical issues in the firm-employee relation, Employing people worldwide: the ethical challenges of globalization.
Consumers and Business Ethics: Consumers as stakeholders, Ethical issues, marketing, and the consumer, Globalization and consumers: the ethical challenges of the global marketplace, Consumers and corporate citizenship: consumer sovereignty and the politics of purchasing, Consumer sovereignty, Ethical consumption.
Suppliers, Competitors and Business Ethics: Suppliers and competitors as stakeholders, Ethical Issues and suppliers, Ethical issues and competitors, Globalization, suppliers, and competitors: the ethical challenges of global business networks, The corporate citizen in the business community: ethical sourcing and fair trade.
Civil Society and Business Ethics: Introduction: what is civil society? Civil society organizations as stakeholders, Ethical issues and CSOs, Globalization and civil society organizations, Corporate citizenship and civil society: charity, collaboration, or regulation? Civil society, business, and sustainability.
Government, Regulations and Business Ethics: Government as a stakeholder, Ethical issues in the relation between business and government, Globalization and business-government relations, Corporate citizenship and regulation: business as a key player in the regulatory game.
Marks: 100
Credit: 3 hours
(Written: 75, Internal Evaluation: 20, Viva: 05)Entrepreneurship skills accelerate economic growth by transforming a population burden into a demographic dividend. The main motto of offering this course is to inspire students to explore their potential to become entrepreneurs and to equip them with guiding principles to become effective entrepreneurs. In order to attain learning outcomes, the course has been designed considering the basic principles of entrepreneurship, the state of global environmental forces, and the dynamics of the Bangladeshi entrepreneurial environment. It covers knowledge and skills about identifying new business ideas, assessing the feasibility of those ideas, preparing a business plan, starting new ventures, conducting managerial operations, maintaining growth, and facing emerging business challenges effectively and efficiently.
Introduction to Entrepreneurship: What is entrepreneurship? Why become an entrepreneur? Characteristics of successful entrepreneurs; Importance of entrepreneurship; The entrepreneurial process; Approaches to entrepreneurship development; What is corporate entrepreneurship? The nature of corporate entrepreneurship; The need for corporate entrepreneuring; Corporate venturing obstacles; Conceptualizing corporate entrepreneurship strategy.
(Case Study: 1.1 - Basic Text: Bruce R. Barringer, 4th Edition)
Recognizing Opportunities and Generating Ideas: Identifying and recognizing opportunities; Finding gaps in the marketplace; Techniques for generating ideas; Encouraging and protecting new ideas.
(Case Study: 2.2 - Basic Text: Bruce R. Barringer)
Feasibility Analysis: Product/service feasibility analysis; Industry/target market feasibility analysis; Organizational feasibility analysis; Financial feasibility analysis.
(Case Study: 3.2 - Basic Text: Bruce R. Barringer)
Writing a Business Plan: Reasons for writing a business plan; Who reads the business plan? Guidelines for writing a business plan; Outline of the business plan; Presenting the business plan to investors.
(Case Study: 4.1 - Basic Text: Bruce R. Barringer)
Developing an Effective Business Model: The importance and diversity of business models; How business models emerge; Potential fatal flaws of business models; Components of an effective business model.
(Case Study: 5.1 - Basic Text: Bruce R. Barringer)
Entrepreneurship Development through Small Business: Modern small business enterprises; Setting up a small business enterprise; Strategic management in small business; Family business.
(Case Study: 6.1 - Basic Text: Bruce R. Barringer)
Ethical, Legal and Financing Issues: Initial ethical and legal issues facing a new firm; Obtaining business licenses and permits; The importance of intellectual property; Patents; Trademarks; Copyrights; Trade secrets; Financial objectives of a firm; The process of financial management.
(Case Study: 7.2 & 12.2 - Basic Text: Bruce R. Barringer)
Building a New Venture Team and Getting Funding: Creating a new venture team; The role of professional advisers; The importance of getting funding for a new venture; Sources of funding.
(Case Study: 9.1 & 10.1 - Basic Text: Bruce R. Barringer)
Managing and Growing an Entrepreneurial Firm: Preparing for growth; Reasons for growth; Managing growth; Challenges of growth; Internal growth strategies; External growth strategies.
(Case Study: 14.1 - Basic Text: Bruce R. Barringer)
Contemporary Issues in Entrepreneurship: Women entrepreneurship; Social entrepreneurship; Challenges of entrepreneurship development in Bangladesh; Role of government and non-government institutions in entrepreneurship development in Bangladesh; Role of Information and Communication Technology (ICT) in entrepreneurship development.
Assignments: Two (on any of the contents)
Bruce R. Barringer and R. Duane Ireland – Entrepreneurship: Successfully Launching New Ventures (4th Edition) Donald F. Kuratko – Introduction to Entrepreneurship (8th Edition)
Poornima M. Charantimath – Entrepreneurship Development and Small Business Enterprises (1st Edition)
Tahlil Azim – Entrepreneurship Development
Marks: 100
Credit: 3 hours
(Written: 75, Internal Evaluation: 20, Viva: 05)This course focuses on the foundation of individual and group behavior at work. Special emphasis is given on the psychological roots, causes, and consequences of human behavior in organizations. Major topics include dynamics of individual and group behavior, motivation, leadership, power and politics, conflict management, and organizational change and stress management.Upon successful completion of the course, students will be able to:
Apply organizational behavior concepts to individual work experiences.
Recognize how different personalities and other issues with different experiences may perceive and react to situations in different ways.
Examine own behavior and beliefs about managerial behavior and to contrast and integrate these ideas with the theories and observations of others.
Work more effectively in a group and team environment.
Learn how organizational power and politics have been exercised.
Understand how organizational conflicts and negotiation have to be managed.
Learn how organizational change and stress have to be managed. Introduction: Meaning and Concepts of Organizational Behavior (OB); Contemporary Issues in OB; Disciplines that Contribute to the OB Field; Challenges and Opportunities for OB; OB Model; Global Implications.
Diversity in Organizations: Diversity; Discrimination; Biographical Characteristics; Ability; Implementing Diversity Management Strategies; Global Implications.
Attitudes and Job Satisfaction: Attitudes; Main components of Attitudes; Major Job Attitudes; Job Satisfaction; Measuring Job Satisfaction; Global Implications.
Emotions and Moods: What are Emotions and Moods; Emotional Labor; Affective Events Theory; Emotional Intelligence; OB Applications of Emotions and Moods; Global Implications.
Personality and Values: What is Personality; The Myers-Briggs Type Indicator; The Big Five Personality Model; Values; The Importance of Values; Linking an Individual's Personality and Values to the workplace; Global implications.
Perception and Individual Decision Making: What is Perception? Person Perception: Making Judgments About Others; The link Between Perception and Individual Decision Making; Decision Making in Organizations; Influences on Decision Making: Individual Differences and Organizational Constraints; Global Implications.
Motivation: From Concepts to Application: Motivating by Job Design; Employee Involvement; Other Concurrent Methods to Motivate Employees.
Foundations of Group Behavior and Understanding Work Teams: Defining and Classifying Groups, Stages of Group Development, Group Properties, Group Decision Making; Differences Between Groups and Teams; Types of Teams; Creating Effective Teams; Turning Individuals into Team Players, Global implications; Relevant Case Analysis.
Leadership: Why Leadership; Charismatic Leadership and Transformational Leadership; Authentic Leadership; Mentoring; Challenges to the Leadership Construct; Creating Effective Leaders; Relevant Case Analysis.
Power and Politics: Definition of Power; Contrasting Leadership and Power; Bases of Power; Dependency the key to Power; Power Tactics; Politics, Causes and Consequences of Political Behavior; Global Implications; Relevant Case Analysis.
Conflict and Negotiations: Definition of Conflict; Transitions in Conflict Thought; Conflict Process; Negotiation; Negotiation Process; Global Implications; Relevant Case Analysis.
Organizational Change and Stress Management: Change; Resistance to Change; Approaches to Managing Organizational Change; Creating a Culture for Change; What is Stress; Source of Stress; Consequences of Stress; Managing Stress; Relevant Case Analysis. S.P. Robbins & Others – Organizational Behavior (Latest Indian Edition) Keith Davis and John Newstrom – Human Behavior at Work: Organizational Behavior
Fred Luthans – Organizational Behavior
J.M. Ivancevich, R. Konopaske, and M.T. Matteson – Organizational Behavior and Management (McGraw Hill, Current Edition)
A.A. Arif and M.A. Taher – Organizational Behavior
Joseph Reitz – Behavior in Organizations
Marks: 100
Credit: 3 hours
(Written: 75, Internal Evaluation: 20, Viva: 05)The general objective of this course is to understand and evaluate Bangladesh as an emerging country along with its economic growth and development. There are a number of specific objectives of starting this course in the BBA program, such as:
To have a clear idea about the history of Bangladesh as an emerging country.
To investigate the diverse constituents of the business environment of Bangladesh.
To critically evaluate fundamental economic development theories and models.
To understand the development strategies and policies for enhancing economic progress. History of Bangladesh: People of Bangladesh, origin and Anthropological identity; Territories of Bangladesh in Ancient Time; Muslim Rule and British Colonial Rule in Indian Sub-Continent; The Pakistan Movement and the Birth of Pakistan; Language Movement, 1948-52; Juktafront Elections, 1954 and Six Point Program, 1966; Mass Upsurge of 1969 and Elections, 1970; and Birth of Bangladesh.
Physical and Natural Resources of Bangladesh: Physical Features of Bangladesh: Location, Climate and major Physiographic Units; Surface Water Inflow and River Systems of Bangladesh; Pattern of Agriculture and types of Forest in Bangladesh; Mineral Resources, Energy, Transportation and Communication, Industrial Setup in Bangladesh; Distribution of Population in Bangladesh.
Political, Administrative and Legal Structure of Bangladesh: Forms of Government and State Mechanism (Executive, Legislatures, Judiciary) of Bangladesh; Constitutions of Bangladesh and its Amendments; Administrative Structure of Bangladesh; Political Parties of Bangladesh; Different Political Regimes of Bangladesh.
Socio-culture of Bangladesh: Social Structure and Social Stratification in Bangladesh; Population Migration from Rural to Urban areas in Bangladesh; Ethnic Minorities in Bangladesh; Social and Cultural Problems of Bangladesh. Concepts and Approaches of Economics of Development: Meaning of Economic Development; Economic Development vs Economic Growth; Measurement of Economic Development; Alternative Measures of Economic Development, PQLI and HDI; Determinants of Development; Challenges to economic Development; Factors of Economic Growth.
Development Theories and Growth Models: An overview of the General Theories of Development; Theories of Economic Development: the Classical Theory, the Schumpeterian Theory, Rostow's Stages of Economic Growth, the Marxian Theory, Big-push theory, Lewis' Theory; Growth Models: The Solow Model, The Harrod-Domar Model, The golden rule of accumulation.
Fundamental Issues and Strategies of Economic Development of Bangladesh: Fundamental Issues: Poverty, Balance of Payment, NGO Activities, Micro Credit Programs, Income Inequality, Unemployment, Inflation, Blue economy, Financial Administration; Strategies: Five year plans, SDG, Technology Transfer, and Women Empowerment.
Local and International Measures for Economic Development: Local Measures: Capital formation, Role of agriculture and industry, Fiscal policy and monetary policy, Deficit financing as an investment, population growth, Entrepreneurship Development; International Measures: Role of foreign trade, Debt problem of LDCs, NIEO, Foreign capital and aid, Foreign Direct Investment (FDI), Role of MNCs.
Comparative Analysis of Developed countries and Lessons for Bangladesh: Comparative analysis of USA, CANADA, UK, GERMANY, JAPAN, FRANCE and CHINA; lessons for the developing and less developing countries and Bangladesh. Prof. Sirajul Islam (Ed.) – Banglapedia: National Encyclopedia of Bangladesh (Latest Edition, 2006)
Sirajul Islam – History of Bangladesh: Social and Cultural History, Political History, Economical History
A.F. Salauddin Ahmed (Ed.) – Bangladesh National Cultural and Heritage: An Introductory Reader
M. L. Jhingan – The Economics of Development and Planning (Revised edition)
Debraj Ray – Development Economics (Princeton University Press, 1998) Michael P. Todaro – Economic Development in the Third World (7th Edition, Longman)
G.M. Meier – Leading Issues in Economic Development (7th Edition)
M. Gillis et al. – Economics of Development (2nd Edition, W.W. Norton, 1985)
S. Ghatak – An Introduction to Development Economics (2nd Edition, Allen and Unwin, 1986)
R. L. Meier – Development Planning (McGraw Hill, 1995)
M. P. Todaro – Development planning: Models and Methods (Oxford University Press, 1971)
Akhtar Hossain – The Economics of Development and Planning (30th Edition, Konark, New Delhi)
CPD (Centre for Policy Dialogue) – Various Publications

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar
